entry.title: Connecting IAS with Decentralized Identity Verification (DIV) (Part 3)
entry.content:
In Part 1, the role of SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the central identity layer for Decentralized Identity Verification (DIV) was introduced.
In Part 2, an IAS tenant was created and made accessible on SAP BTP.

This article completes the setup by connecting the IAS tenant to DIV, explaining how authentication and authorization are enforced, and showing how administrators and end users are granted access to DIV provisioning and wallet tenants.

 

Authentication and Authorization Overview

Once IAS is connected to DIV, all access to DIV components is mediated by IAS. This applies to both:

  • DIV Provisioning UI (administrative access)
  • DIV Wallet Tenants (end-user access)

During authentication, IAS performs two checks:

  1. Authentication by verifying the user’s identity via the federated corporate identity provider
  2. Authorization by validating whether the authenticated user is allowed to access the requested DIV tenant or application

Only if both checks succeed access is granted.

This model ensures that authentication and authorization decisions are consistently enforced across the DIV components.

 

Step 1: Starting DIV Provisioning via SAP for Me

The connection between DIV and IAS is established during the DIV provisioning process in SAP for Me.

In SAP for Me, navigate to Systems & Provisioning → Provisioning

The Provisioning tab lists all systems that can be provisioned for the account. In the section “Systems to be Provisioned”, a Start Provisioning button is available (Figure 1).

Selecting this option initiates the provisioning workflow for DIV.

Figure 1: SAP for Me - Systems & Provisioning - Provisioning

 

During the provisioning process for DIV, an IAS tenant must be selected. The provisioning screen displays the available SAP Cloud Identity Services tenants. This might be the URL created during the previous blog article.

Typically, the default IAS tenant associated with the BTP global account is selected (Figure 2). Once confirmed, the provisioning request can be submitted. After a short processing time, the provisioning status changes from requested to provided. The DIV Provisioning Service becomes available and is automatically linked to the selected IAS tenant.

Figure 2: SAP for Me - Start New Provisioning Request

 

Step 2: IAS Application Created for DIV

As part of the provisioning process, an IAS application representing the DIV service is automatically created and linked to the corresponding BTP subaccount. This application functions as the technical anchor point for all authentication and authorization decisions related to DIV.

In the IAS Admin Console, this application appears under Applications & Resources, grouped under the relevant BTP subaccount (Figure 3).

Figure 3: DIV application automatically created in the IAS Admin Console and linked to the corresponding SAP BTP subaccount.

 

Step 3: Restricting Application Access in IAS

By default, IAS applications can be configured with different access modes. For enterprise DIV deployments, access must be restricted.

In the IAS Admin Console:

  1. Open the DIV application (Figure 4)
  2. Navigate to the Authentication and Access tab
  3. Select Private access mode

The Private option ensures that:

  • Users cannot self-register
  • Only users explicitly imported or provisioned are allowed to authenticate

This setting is mandatory for controlled, enterprise-grade DIV deployments.

Figure 4: Configuration of application access mode for the DIV application in IAS

 

Step 4: Authorization Model for DIV

IAS enforces authorization separately for:

  • DIV Provisioning UI
  • DIV Wallet Tenants

This separation enables fine-grained access control and clear separation of duties.

 

DIV Provisioning Authorization

The following user groups are created in the IAS tenant by default:

  • DIV_Provisioning_Admin

  • DIV_Provisioning_Viewer

Only users assigned to these groups are able to access provisioning functions such as tenant creation, configuration, and user management.

 

DIV Wallet Authorization

For wallet access, IAS restricts users to specific wallet tenants. Even if authentication succeeds, access is denied if the user is not authorized for the requested tenant.

This prevents lateral access between departments or partner organizations in multi-tenant environments.

 

Step 5: Importing Users and Assigning Roles in IAS

Users must be explicitly imported into IAS and assigned to the appropriate DIV-related groups.

In the IAS Admin Console:

  1. Navigate to Users & Authorizations
  2. Select the DIV application
  3. Choose Import Users and upload a CSV file (Figure 5)

The CSV file contains user attributes such as:

  • Status (mandatory)
  • First name
  • Last name (mandatory)
  • Email address (mandatory)
  • Group memberships

More CSV file attributes are listed and described on the SAP Cloud Identity Services help page.

An example CSV file includes a groups column containing DIV-specific roles (Table 1). These group assignments determine whether a user receives provisioning access, wallet access, or both.

Figure 5: User and authorization management for the DIV application in the IAS Admin Console, including user import via CSV

 

statusfirstNamelastNamemailgroups
activeJohnDoejohn.doe@sap.com"DIV_System_Administrator, DIV_Application_Administrator" 
activeJaneDoejane.doe@sap.com"DIV_Application_User"

Table 1: Example CSV file used to import users into IAS with assigned DIV-specific authorization groups

 

Step 6: Authentication and Access Enforcement

When a user attempts to access a DIV component, IAS performs an authorization check.

For example, when accessing the DIV Provisioning UI, the user is first redirected to IAS. The authentication screen displays the associated BTP subaccount and requests credentials (Figure 7).

Figure 7: IAS authentication and authorization check triggered when accessing the DIV Provisioning UI

 

Only if:

  • Authentication via the corporate identity provider succeeds, and
  • The user belongs to a group authorized for the requested application,

is access granted and the user redirected back to DIV.

 

Resulting Architecture and Security Model

After completing the steps described above, the following architecture and security characteristics are in place:

  • IAS acts as the single authentication entry point for all DIV components
  • Corporate identity providers remain the source of user identities
  • Authorization is centrally enforced through IAS groups and role assignments
  • Administrative and end-user access paths are clearly separated at the application level
  • Multi-tenant isolation is consistently enforced

The trust relationship between the SAP BTP subaccount and IAS is established during provisioning and configuration. At runtime, IAS issues authentication tokens containing group claims. These tokens are validated directly by the DIV applications based on the preconfigured trust.

The result is a secure, scalable, and enterprise-ready DIV setup aligned with real-world organizational structures (Figure 8).

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Figure 8: High-level authentication and authorization flow for DIV using IAS

 

💡This concludes the three-part series on integrating SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication with Decentralized Identity Verification.

 

Read more

⬅️   Go back to Creating and Accessing an IAS Tenant on SAP BTP (Part 2)

⬅️⬅️   Go back to Why IAS Is Essential for Enterprise DIV Implementations (Part 1)

 

  • Introduction to SAP Decentralized Identity Verification (DIV) and Related SSI Blog Posts 
  • Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI): A Usage Catalyst for Data Spaces 
  • Feature Scope Description for Decentralized Identity Verification (DIV help page)
  • What Are Cloud Identity Services? (IAS help page)
  • Import or Update Users for a Specific Application (IAS help page)

entry.title: Connecting SAP S/4HANA On-Prem Directly to Advanced Event Mesh using Cloud Connector - finally!
entry.content:
With Package Integration 1.4.0 (December 2025), the Advanced Event Mesh Adapter for SAP Integration Suite delivers several noteworthy updates.

One of these has been requested repeatedly by customers, partners, and SAP architects working in hybrid landscapes:
official support for on-premise connectivity via the SAP Cloud Connector.

In addition to this key enhancement, the release also includes other technical changes, such as an update to the default acknowledgment mode.

What’s New in Package Integration 1.4.0?

1️⃣On-Premise Connectivity via SAP Cloud Connector

The AEM Adapter can now officially route traffic to on-premise systems through the SAP Cloud Connector.

This enables a supported hybrid architecture where:

S/4HANA on-prem produces events
SAP AEM distributes them
Network security remains intact

Important note from the official release notes:

Avoid mixing proxy configurations within the same tenant to prevent unexpected routing behavior.

This is especially critical in complex hybrid tenants.

2️⃣Change of Default Acknowledgment Mode

The default acknowledgment mode has changed from:
      Automatic Immediate
to  Automatic On Exchange Complete

Technical implications:

  • Clearer delivery semantics

  • improved error handling
  • Better alignment with enterprise-grade integration scenarios

Package Integration 1.4.0 introduces multiple technical improvements.
The Cloud Connector support for the Advanced Event Mesh Adapter is a long-requested and architecturally significant enhancement.

It enables a clean, supported reference architecture to connect SAP S/4HANA on-premise directly to SAP Advanced Event Mesh, using SAP Cloud Connector as the secure hybrid backbone.

For SAP architects, this removes a major blocker when designing modern, event-driven hybrid landscapes.

https://api.sap.com/package/AdvancedEventMeshAdapterforSAPIntegrationSuite/documents

entry.title: SAP BTP - IS - ( IA | CPI ) - The Commander's Blueprint: Rebuilding B2B EDI Governance with SAP BTP.
entry.content:
Spoiler

Disclaimer: This article is written for Integration Architects, Solution Architects, B2B Leads, Managers, and Integration Consultants dealing with B2B EDI modernization to SAP BTP Integration Suite. It focuses on governance, strategy, not in quick wins.

Instead, it is a strategic orientation on the mindset and professional positioning required of an SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant (IA + CPI).

My goal is to provide a strategic guide on how to approach a scalable solution and lead a business transformation, rather than just explaining how to configure isolated components without considering the entire ecosystem.

Please note: This is not a "click-by-click" tutorial or a step-by-step technical manual.

Introduction

In my previous article, SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant - Part IV - I outlined a path for integration professionals to evolve beyond the “CPI Developer” label toward true SAP BTP Integration Suite specialization.

Today, I want to put this into practice by discussing a critical scenario: the modernization of B2B/EDI processes in S/4HANA projects.

Many companies adopting Rise with SAP and Clean Core methodologies still make the strategic mistake of maintaining legacy platforms (such as Seeburger or OpenText) or, even worse, they attempt to rebuild decades of EDI logic within massive and manual CPI iFlows. The result is what I call the "EDI-T-REX Legacy": a heavy, undocumented architecture that no one dares to touch.

SAP BTP Integration Advisor (IA) is the tool that breaks this cycle, transforming what would be a "Frankenstein" of code into clean governance, where the functional team regains control over what is being mapped.

My journey with Integration Advisor has been one of deep technical discovery. Until recently, it was an area I had not explored in depth — which makes the insights shared here the result of focused study and hands-on experimentation rather than inherited project patterns.

Quick Project Setup B2B EDI

Let me show you an imaginary project conversation.

The following imaginary conversation highlights a common mistake: treating SAP BTP Integration Advisor as an afterthought rather than the core of your EDI governance and structural framework.

Enterprise Architect / Solution Architect

Our next challenge: global B2B EDI migration with ~20 international partners across legacy platforms.

We're dealing with a chaotic mix of standards (EDIFACT, ANSI X12, VDA, RosettaNet), each with partner-specific deviations.

The real question: Are we migrating platforms or just moving decades of undocumented business semantics?

Will we implement a true semantic layer or replicate "partner-to-partner spaghetti" in a modern tool ?

Who actually owns these standards and partner lifecycle today in SAP BTP Integration Suite ?

SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Commander 

OK I understand your worry, my proposal is rebuilding semantic governance, not just migrating EDI flows.

Using SAP Integration Advisor's MIG objects, we centralize business semantics. MAGs automated rules mapping transformation.

TPM shifts ownership from people to platform every contract becomes a managed asset for seamless partner onboarding.

Reality check: Integration Advisor exposes our weaknesses. 

Enterprise Architect / Solution Architect

Clear asnwer thank you but how do we avoid adapter hell (HTTPS, AS2, SFTP, OFTP2, X.400)?

Number range  collisions across partners, hidden Groovy "quick fixes" for encoding/delimiters, SAP API Management boundary: REST wrappers vs. batch mindset in case that is needed ?

BTP concerns: Handling Functional Acknowledgments (997/CONTRL) without doubling message volume.

Support teams already drown in dashboards APIM + TPM adds complexity.

Single Pane of monitoring ?

SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Commander

Use SAP CPI use standard EDI Integration Templates and TPM V2—not custom iFlows.

Place APIM in front for quota management and spike control in case that is needed and only to partners that use API https or Soap.

SAP BTP manages ICN per partner, preventing collisions. Integration Advisor handles delimiters/encoding—no Groovy hacks. Status correlation links acknowledgments to original messages, avoiding dashboard clutter.

Monitoring: Application Correlation IDs track payloads and B2B and CPI monitoring centralized in B2B Monitoring in SAP CPI.

Legacy protocols (X.400, OFTP2): Third-party adapters only when needed. Maximize native Integration Suite for Clean Core architecture.

Enablement: I'll personally train the EDI ops team on MIGs, MAGs, and TPM.

Bottom line: Platform complexity handled. You focus on strategy; I'll deliver the engine and team.

Enterprise Architect / Solution Architect

Fair enough. That is exactly the level of technical clarity and leadership I was looking for to validate the viability of this project. You’ve addressed the architectural risks and, more importantly, the human element of this migration.

Phased approach: Start with 2 strategic vendors and 2 EDI formats to build our "Golden Template." This establishes confidence in SAP Integration Advisor (MIG, MAG, TPM) without overwhelming operations.

Your mission: Prepare the platform and enablement materials. Align the front-line team with your vision.

Let's start to think in possible remove the "T-Rex" and enter a new governance era. Time to execute.

Thank you for your support—let’s get to work.

The "T-Rex" Scenario: Why the fear of change is your greatest risk?

In the image below, I describe what I call the "Legacy Trap Mindset." It is the famous "T-Rex": an EDI ecosystem that no one wants to touch because "it has been working for 10 years."

The problem is that no one knows where the documentation is, how the transformations were built, or how to perform quick maintenance in case of an error. Within the context of Clean Core, keeping this dinosaur alive creates an innovation bottleneck.

Image 1 - trap mindste legacy for B2B EDI

A SAP BTP Integration Suite - IA Perspective: Where does Integration Advisor truly fit?

Although IA is underutilized in the market, the hands-on mastery I have acquired by studying this tool in depth allows me to face this chaos with a clear strategy. If I were leading this migration today, my stance as an SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant would be structured on two pillars:

Enablement and Trust (The Human Factor)

I would provide a practical internal course to give confidence to the business team, B2B specialists, and SAP functional consultants. They need to understand that IA is not just another "IT tool," but a platform that returns mapping control to those who truly understand the business rules. It leverages Machine Learning and SAP’s Crowd Encyclopedia to accelerate what used to take weeks of manual coding.

Governance and Architecture (The Technical Factor)

This is where many fail: creating MIGs (Message Implementation Guidelines) and MAGs (Mapping Guidelines) in isolation only speeds up technical construction but doesn't solve the underlying disorder. My role would be:

  • Defining Semantics: Creating MIGs with real business meaning.
  • The "Canonical Path": Structuring the integration using a canonical model ( When two types of EDI message type are semantic with the respective message type from SAP side)
  • Materialization via TPM: Demonstrating how Trading Partner Management (TPM) must be used to materialize integration templates and contracts.

Without alignment between the contracts defined in TPM and the mappings in IA, the most valuable assets are lost: governance, traceability, and reusability. It is not about "copying and pasting" legacy logic, but using IA to clean the architecture—leaving SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) only with lightweight technical execution and lean iFlows.

“MIG and MAG build - TPM governs - Without TPM, there is no sustainable B2B integration.” 

Image 2 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Internal Enablement on SAP Integration Advisor.

References
  • SAP BTP Integration Advisor Demo
  • How to build custom B2B Interface using Integration Advisor , AS2 Adapters and API Proxy
  • Integration Advisor: Overview of components for building B2B integration content and further reading

A SAP BTP Integration Suite - IA Perspective: fit into MIGs?

The first step in killing the "T-Rex" is the correct construction of MIGs (Message Implementation Guidelines). As a consultant, my role is to guide the team at the base of the pyramid:

  • 1:1 Structure: Creating one object per Message Type (EDI or SAP), ensuring granularity.
  • Business Semantics: Going beyond the technical side by defining rules, field usage, and documenting Codelists.
  • Facilitation: Acting as the bridge that translates technical standards (EDIFACT/X12) into the actual requirements of the SAP process.

Responsibilities and Roles of the SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - IA - MIG

What the IA Consultant DOES:

  • Facilitation: Teaches how to operate the IA and apply the correct semantics to fields.
  • Technical Oversight: Ensures the MIG follows the standard (1:1) and remains technically reusable.
  • Mentorship: Guides the documentation of codelists and qualifiers.

What the IA Consultant DOES NOT do:

  • Define Business Rules: The business decides what it needs; the consultant only shows where to implement it.
  • Data Governance: The consultant delivers the structure, but responsibility for the data itself lies with the process owner.
  • Flow Decisions: Does not interpret commercial rules without validation from Key Users.

“MIG defines clarity, semantics, and standards."

Image 3 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Providing guidance on where and how to create a MIG and select the message type.

References - MIG
  • Creating MIGs and MAGs Using Integration Advisor in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • integration content advisor: Create a customized interface using MIG editor
  • Integration Advisor – multi-payload support for MIG creation
  • Integration Advisor – direct support of EDI payloads
  • Creating MIGs and MAGs Using Integration Advisor in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • integration content advisor: Discover B2B/A2A standard libraries
  • Integration Advisor : Use of cXML Type System Exporting to CPI

A SAP BTP Integration Suite - IA Perspective: fit into MAG?

If the MIG is the definition of the object, the MAG (Mapping Guideline) is where the transformation magic happens. The consultant acts as the guide who materializes this connection.

What the Consultant DOES:

  • Connectivity: Guides the correct selection of Source and Target MIGs.
  • Targeted Mapping: Explains how to perform field-to-field mapping, ensuring that the semantics defined in the MIG are respected.
  • Conversion Logic: Implements and teaches how to use basic conversion functions to adjust data between standards (e.g., transforming date formats or units of measure).
  • Artifact Generation: Demonstrates how to generate the schemas (XSD) and transformations (XSLT) that the CPI will utilize.

What the Consultant DOES NOT do:

  • Define Complex Business Logic: The consultant implements the technical conversion, but calculation rules or commercial decisions must come from the functional side.
  • Map Without a Base: The consultant must not start a MAG if the MIGs have not been properly validated by the business.

“The MAG is not a technical mapping; it is an adapter between meaning and the platform.”

Image 4 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - How to create a MAG from MIG outbound and MIG Inbound

References MAG
  • Integration Advisor: complex mapping code for dimension conversion in SAP B2B
  • Base-/Overlay Mappings for Integration Advisor
  • Integration Advisor – Download MAG Simulation Result as EDI Payload
  • Accessing Headers and Properties in Integration Advisor (IA) Mapping Guidelines (MAG) with the Global Parameters
  • Integration Advisor: Conditional Mapping Feature
  • Integration Advisor: Conditional mapping in MAG to pass one value from the multiple occurrence of an element, based on other element's value
  • Complex Mappings 
    • SAP MAG mapping many occurrence node to 1 occurrence node and take last occurrence values to target
    • SAP TPM MAG or IA MAG :choose or multiple if conditions
    • SAP TPM MAG conditional mapping target node it's child

Where does an SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant fit into TPM?

The SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant must guide and enforce the use of TPM (Trading Partner Management) as a true governance layer, to be honest, TPM is not a "ten-headed beast" or some unreachable diamond; however, the most critical part is the definition of Identifiers within the EDI envelopes. If these are configured incorrectly, the agreement will fail to trigger even if everything else is perfect. I will demonstrate this below.

What is NOT the responsibility of the (IA/TPM) Consultant

For a migration to be sustainable, the consultant must not take over tasks that belong to business management or day-to-day operations.

The Consultant DOES NOT:

  • Define Commercial Rules: Does not decide legal terms, freight conditions, or SLAs. The business defines the rule; the consultant only configures it.
  • Operational Execution (TPM): Is not responsible for the continuous registration of hundreds of partners, certificate maintenance, or agreement versioning once the project has stabilized.
  • Contract Negotiation: Does not act in the alignment of deadlines or contractual obligations with external suppliers and customers.

Summary: The consultant designs the architecture and the migration process. The operational life of the tool (onboarding new partners and business decisions) must be managed by the company's internal EDI specialist team.

“MIG defines the meaning, MAG defines the destination, TPM defines the contract.”

Image 5 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - How to create a TPM

References
  • TPM Onboarding Demo
  • Use TPM, IA, CI to Efficiently Manage and Run Complex B2B Transactions
  • Bridging EDI and SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Seamless EDI-to-SOAP Integration

The Developer’s Playground: Where SAP CPI Experts Take Control

Now, we enter the territory where we, as SAP CPI experts, have total dominance.

Once all the steps above are completed—specifically once the TPM is configured and the Agreement is accepted and deployed—something critical happens behind the scenes.

We aren't just "connecting pipes" anymore; we are orchestrating a dynamic, metadata-driven engine.

Image 6 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - CPI Implementation

Technical Deep Dive: Standard Packages and the "Plain ID" Trap

I will now explain the possibilities and the path I took to streamline this integration. I am intentionally ignoring the option of building a fully manual, custom iFlow. Such an approach would be unnecessarily complex, time-consuming, and redundant given the standard packages available.

Standard EDI Package Options:

EDI Integration Templates for SAP Integration Advisor - A versatile set of iFlows for various scenarios.

Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2- The complete governance suite.

The TPM V2 Workflow:

  • Step 1: Ingress (Generic, depends on the channel: HTTP, SFTP, SOAP, IDoc, ProcessDirect, AS2).
  • Step 1b: Persistency (Write Message to Message Queue).
  • Step 2: Interchange Processing Flow V2 (The "Brain").
  • Step 3: Receiver Communication Flow V2.

My Assessment: After debugging every step in both packages, the standalone EDI Integration Templates work perfectly if you have your MIGs and MAGs ready, standalone iFlow is only valid for small-scale integrations.

Image 6 - Inject Process MAG to SAP CPI Iflow

The "Isolation Test" Strategy: Validating the MAG

Pro-Tip: I used the standalone iFlow to validate my mapping (MAG) before moving to TPM, injecting the MAG directly into a copy of the standard iFlow and testing with a Content Modifier using plain text (X12 850), I could validate the logic instantly. This is crucial because if you try to debug the Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow without a prior test, grab a coffee and bring plenty of patience and papers because you are going to cry as me.

Crucial Recommendation: Pay close attention to the Validate and Analyze EDI Interchange step configuration. I suggest mirroring the settings in Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow V2 exactly.

How to do it:

  • Configure the Pre and Post-processing steps with your generated XSD and XSLT files.
  • Run a test using a Content Modifier with the raw EDI "N" format (in my case, an X12 850) as plain text.
  • It doesn’t matter if it’s EDI to IDoc or, as I tested, EDI to OData—the communication channel is secondary at this stage.

Note on Testing: Please keep in mind that I am an expert in integration, not in business semantics or specific EDI file structures. Many of my test files were generated manually or extracted from internet, which caused significant issues during the validation phase.

Image 7 - Validacao da MAG e verificado do EDI Splitter

The "Agreement Plain ID" Headache

Once everything is configured and the agreement is deployed in TPM, it generates a unique "Information Package" for the trading partner based on identifiers. This is where the Agreement Plain ID comes in the configuration of identifiers in the Company and Partners regards the exchange header validation.

Imagem 8 - Aggremment Active in TPM

After successfully completing these steps, you will generate the "Trading Partner Information Package." This process creates a unique identification string based on the company's and the partner's identifiers.

This object—the Agreement Plain ID—is exactly what caused me so much frustration during the implementation.

What does a string like SOAP-ASC-X12--004010-BUYERCO-ZZ----SAP-ZZ----850 actually mean? It is a concatenation of the identifiers found in the EDI header:

  • SOAP: Partner Sender Channel
  • ASC-X12--004010: Message Type
  • BUYERCO-ZZ: Partner Identifier
  • SAP-ZZ: Receiver Identifier
  • 850: Transaction Code (Orders)

The Trap: If these identifiers are registered incorrectly in Company and Partner sides —even by one character—the integration will fail. There is a Groovy script of nearly 1,000 lines inside the standard package that handles this logic. I had to debug it to understand why my agreement wasn't being found.

Forced error: ISA*00*...*ZZ*BUYERCO*ZZ*SAP2... (The "SAP2" mismatch prevents the iFlow from finding the Agreement in the TPM table).

 
Imagem 9 - Bug between Partner Agremment ID and the header detail of the message as EDI or SAP Message Type

Crucial: Number Range Synchronization Ensure the Number Range is identically named in the TPM Company/Partner, the Agreement, and the SAP CPI Number Range monitor. Failure to align these exactly will trigger errors. I also experienced a platform bug where a locked Agreement prevented the deletion/update of the INC_UN_EDIFACT range.

 

Image 10 - Number Range

Pro Tip: Custom Number Ranges If a TPM Agreement gets stuck (e.g., locking INC_INB_EDIFACT), use a Custom Number Range. This bypasses platform bugs and is a best practice for high-volume scenarios to prevent duplicate sequence numbers across different partner agreements, remember that this was test with ODETTE EDIFACT 96A not with another scenario X12.

In the platform that I have access there is somehow to agreements that was not delete 100% locking the INC_INB_EDIFACT, I have no idea how to delete or if must open SAP ticket for it:

 

Image 11 - Trading Agrement locking the INC_INB_EDIFACT.
SAP CPI Iflow - Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow V2 - for SAP TPM - SAP Integration Advisor and always failed in this step:

 

Image 12 - Set Control Numers fails

Error: javax.script.ScriptException: java.lang.Exception: java.lang.IllegalStateException: Error reading number range ICN_UN_EDIFACT @ line 46 in setControlNumbers.groovy, cause: java.lang.IllegalStateException: Error reading number range ICN_UN_EDIFACT

I debugged another simple iFlow and it worked, so I still couldn't understand the cause of these locked records. I opened a thread on SAP SCN but received no response. I then tested a workaround—which I do not recommend—that involved modifying the standard CPI iFlow in Step 2, though I later reverted it to the original version.

Image 13 - Workaround Iflow to check outside TPM config the number range ICN_UN_EDIFACT 

Image 13 - Workaround Iflow to check outside TPM lock range number INC_UNB_EDIFACT

Inside the Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow, there is a Local Integration Process for mapping. Since MAGs are based on XSLT and have some limitations, you can use ProcessDirect to call PRE and POST processing flows for complex logic or fine-tuning.

Image 14 - Local Integration Process - PRO and POS mapping processing

Now, let’s look at the "perfect scenario"—everything built, configured, and ready for action. In this case, I decided to push the limits: I used Scenario 1 with a SOAP call executing an EDI Flat File via a SOAP envelope.

The result? Don't worry about Step 5; I am simply looping the message back into the CPI for processing.

Imagem 15 - Full processing
 

Stop Guessing, Start Proving

You don’t have to replace everything at once. The most viable path is a low-risk POC (Proof of Concept). Pick a single, non-critical flow, apply the Integration Advisor methodology, and witness the efficiency firsthand. Once the value is proven, you can execute a robust, confident migration.

Industrialized Automation vs. Manual Labor

The large-scale EDI migrations, tools like int4 Shield (formerly IFTT) and Figaf automate what would be manual labor.

Key benefits:

  • Extract legacy definitions → auto-convert to TPM entries and iFlows
  • Months of development → weeks of configuration
  • Capture real messages → run through new system → automatic comparison
  • Ensures 100% mapping fidelity without partner involvement

Result: Migration becomes governance and audit, not error-prone manual work. Predictable timelines, lean team, no supply chain disruptions.

  • Int4 Shield (formerly IFTT):
    • Int4 Shield - Migration to SAP Integration Suite
    • SAP News - Test Automation for SAP Integration Suite
    • SAP Store - Advanced Business Process Testing
  • Figaf Tool:
    •  Figaf - SAP EDI Migration Tooling
    • Figaf Docs - Connecting to Integration Advisor
    • Figaf Blog - Migrating EDI to SAP Integration Suite

 Image 16 - The Beginning of the T-Rex Extinction 

Conclusion

Modernizing EDI on SAP BTP isn't just moving iFlows from on-premise or cloud it's remove the control from outside to insade with governance and structure.

Using Integration Advisor as the semantic brain and CPI as the agile engine, we transition from technical workarounds to managing true business assets.

Creating strategic content connecting architectural vision with technical execution takes weeks of deep work. If this "Commander's Blueprint" helped you see beyond standard tutorials.

Drop a comment. Share your perspective. LET'S GO!

Best regards,

SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert - Viana

entry.title: cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter: a pragmatic way to cut AI token costs using SAP CAP
entry.content:
If you’ve been experimenting with AI in enterprise apps, you’ve probably felt the "token tax". It’s the silent cost hidden in every JSON payload you stream to an LLM. The more verbose the structure, the more you pay, both in latency and in cost.

This is exactly where the cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter lands: a small, composable CAP plugin that brings the TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) protocol to your services. So you can keep your code, keep your patterns, and still send leaner payloads to AI systems.

In short:

  • It adds TOON as a protocol to CAP (REST and OData variants)
  • It automatically converts JSON ↔ TOON on the wire
  • It can auto-pick the most token-efficient response format
  • It logs token statistics so you can quantify the win

It's is directly inspired by an article I read on medium: "TOON, MCP and Agents: Less Hype, More Real Architecture (and a 25% Saving That Actually Matters)".

Toon procotol adapter in action

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Why Toon in CAP?

Let’s start with the pain:

  • JSON is great for machines and tools, but it’s verbose for LLMs
  • LLM pricing and latency scale with tokens, not bytes
  • Sending full JSON schemas, deeply nested objects, and descriptive keys is comfortable for us, but expensive for models

TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) flips that mindset:

  • It’s a compact, human-readable, model-friendly representation
  • It removes structural noise while keeping semantic meaning
  • It often reduces token counts by 20–50% compared to JSON

What the adapter gives you in CAP:

  • A protocol-level optimization, no refactors in service logic
  • Opt-in per service via @protocol annotations
  • Automatic conversion on responses (JSON → TOON), optional on requests (TOON → JSON)
  • A safety net: it can auto-decide per response if JSON is actually cheaper and choose that instead

So the real "why" is simple: you can introduce AI cost and latency optimizations where they belong, at the boundary, without touching domain logic, entities, or your CAP service handlers.

Where it fits in your architecture

If your CAP service:

  • Feeds agents with app context
  • Exposes APIs to AI tools or MCP-style execution flows

…then the adapter sits right at the edge of your service. Clients can consume Toon endpoints or keep using JSON/OData, your service handlers continue to speak plain JavaScript objects. Meanwhile, the adapter keeps score and trims the fluff.

How to use it (in less than 5 minutes)

1) Install

npm install @geertjanklaps/cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter

Make sure you’re on @sap/cds >= 9.

2) Annotate your service with the Toon protocol

REST-style TOON:

@protocol: ['toon']
service MyToonService {
}

Prefer OData compatibility but want TOON on the wire?

@protocol: ['toon-odata']
service MyToonService {
}

3) Implement your service as usual

NoTOON-specific code needed. No new SDK. Your handlers stay clean.

4) Configure behavior in package.json

{
  "cds": {
    "toonAdapter": {
      "requestProcessingEnabled": false,
      "responseProcessingEnabled": true,
      "logTokenStatistics": true,
      "autoDetermineResponseContentType": true
    }
  }
}

What this means:

  • responseProcessingEnabled: true → your JSON responses can be converted to TOON
  • requestProcessingEnabled: false → clients can still send JSON; turn this on if you want to accept TOON requests too
  • autoDetermineResponseContentType: true → the adapter will send TOON or JSON based on which uses fewer tokens for that response
  • logTokenStatistics: true → you’ll see logs like: [toon] - Token count - JSON: 150, TOON: 95, Difference: 55 (36,7%)

5) Call the endpoints

  • REST base: /toon/
    • Example: GET /toon/my-toon-service/getData
  • OData base: /toon-odata/
    • Example: GET /toon-odata/my-toon-odata-service/getData

By default, clients can still send JSON. If you enable TOON requests, they can POST with Content-Type: text/toon.

When to use it

Ideal scenarios:

  • AI/LLM integrations where every token counts
  • Near-real-time experiences where shaving tokens reduces latency
  • Cost-sensitive workloads or multi-tenant setups where savings compound

Observed benefits:

  • Token reduction: typically 20–50% vs JSON
  • Lower bandwidth usage
  • Reduced AI API cost and faster roundtrips

This is not about "changing your data model for AI", it’s about optimizing the transport layer so your CAP services can collaborate with AI systems efficiently.

Practical tips:

  • Don’t refactor your code. Add the protocol, turn on responseProcessingEnabled, and measure
  • Keep autoDetermineResponseContentType enabled in production. Sometimes JSON is cheaper, let the adapter pick the winner
  • Enable logTokenStatistics in development and load tests. Record baseline token counts before/after
  • If you expose an MCP server using @gavdi/cap-mcp, use the toon-odata protocol

A minimal test you can run today

  1. Enable TOON on a read-heavy service
  2. Turn on logTokenStatistics
  3. Hit endpoints with realistic data (lists, expansions, nested structures)
  4. Compare JSON vs TOON token counts in logs
  5. Forward the same responses to your AI layer and measure end-to-end latency and cost

Expect measurable token drops on non-trivial payloads. In multi-tenant scenarios, those savings add up, quietly and meaningfully.

Closing thoughts

We don’t need more hype around "AI-native" architectures. We need small, composable decisions that make our systems cheaper, faster, and more maintainable. The cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter does exactly that for CAP:

  • No rewrites.
  • Real savings.
  • Better UX for agents and LLMs.

If you’re serious about bringing AI into your SAP landscape, start where it hurts the budget: tokens on the wire. Add TOON at the protocol layer, measure, and move on to the next pragmatic win.

Useful links:

  • NPM: @geertjanklaps/cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter
  • Issues and support: GitHub repository
  • Toon documentation: @toon-format/toon

If you try this in a multi-tenant setup or with MCP-heavy flows, I’d love to hear your numbers!

entry.title: Integrating SAP Business Data Cloud data products with SAP HANA central
entry.content:
Here’s the thing.  Most innovative projects don’t fail because of ideas.  They stall because data is hard to reuse, hard to govern, and expensive to move.

That’s why the new GA integration between SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP HANA Cloud central feels like a real turning point.

In this live session, we’ll show how partners can publish once and consume many times—using zero-copy delta sharing to bring SAP BDC data products straight into SAP HANA Cloud central.  From there, developers can build apps in SAP Business Application Studio, work with HDI containers, and layer in AI using SAP’s AI Core toolkit.

The fun part?  Seeing how it all connects.  Graphs for relationships.  Vectors for semantic search.  Tabular AI for forecasting and anomaly detection.  Plus, a look at what’s coming next, including Joule Agents with direct access to SAP HANA Cloud engines.

If you enjoy building solutions that feel lighter, faster, and just make sense—join us.

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPHANA #SAPDevelopers #SAPAI #SAPPartners

entry.title: SAP RPT‑1: Why is it Essential for Predicting Business Outcomes in Today’s and Future Generative AI?
entry.content:
Ever since the introduction of ChatGPT in November 2022, Generative AI has reshaped the AI industry. Companies like Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and of course SAP have accelerated innovation, recognizing the enormous value Generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) bring to enterprise operations. 

General‑purpose LLMs excel at understanding language, reasoning over text, and identifying patterns in unstructured information. They are creative, adaptive, and capable of leveraging data from files, documents, and multimedia across diverse data systems.

The Motive

However, when it comes to actually predicting business outcomes, LLMs fall short. Why?
Because they are not designed for high‑precision, multi‑step reasoning over large, enterprise‑grade tabular datasets.

And this is where the majority of business data resides: structured tables, such as GL accounts, invoices, inventory, sales records, financial transactions, and countless others. Not in free‑form text.

Everyone wants to be able to predict business outcomes like:

  • What is the probability of converting a sale?
  • Which customers are likely to pay late?
  • Who is at risk of churn?
  • ...
Historically, answering these questions required traditional machine learning like classification models, linear regression, etc. (a.k.a Traditional AI or Narrow AI). The problem with this is that classical ML requires to train a model per task, which easily can lead to hundreds of separate models hard to maintain in the long run, making it very cumbersome, expensive and extremely difficult to scale.
 
 
The Solution: SAP RPT-1
 
To solve this, SAP introduced in TechEd 2025 the first foundation model specifically designed for structured enterprise data: Relational Pre‑Trained Transformer 1 (RPT‑1).  The model is trained natively on tabular business datasets and is engineered to understand rows, columns, joins and business semantics out of the box. As the name suggests, RPT‑1 is:
  • Relational: optimized for structured relational business data
  • Pre‑trained: powered by tens of thousands of GPU hours (no more endless classical ML training cycles)
  • Transformer‑based: performs logical, not linguistic, transformations (e.g., filtering, joining, aggregating, and multi‑step reasoning)

Instead of hundreds of ML models, you now can use one single foundation model for many predictive tasks.

One of the most disruptive features of RPT‑1 is its in‑context learning capability. Instead of training or fine‑tuning multiple modes, you simple provide historical rows of data and request predictions for new rows. Prediction cases like:

  • Customer churn prediction
  • Late delivery prediction
  • Late payment prediction
  • Sales conversion prediction
  • And many more

RPT‑1 Performance Benchmark

RPT-1 outperforms both LLM and classical ML for tabular data and high value agentic cases. Here are some stats so far:

  • 50x faster than LLMs
  • Up to 2x Prediction Quality vs Narrow AI/ML Models
  • Up to 3.5x Prediction Quality vs LLMs
  • 100,000x fewer GPU FLOPs
  • 50,000x Less Energy consumption vs LLMs*

*comparable tasks on a NVIDIA H100, as a benchmark. Sources (TechEd, Blog) .

Why are these stats so important? On top of the benefits you can already imagine like time and resource savings; according to Gartner 40% of agentic-AI projects are likely to be canceled by end of 2027, mainly because of high cost, complexity, or unclear business value (source). RPT‑1 directly addresses these challenges.

 

Sales Conversion Prediction Example Use Case

During SAP TechEd, a compelling demo showcased how RPT‑1 integrates with SAP’s Agentic AI.

Imagine a sales team wanting to prioritize leads based on their likelihood to convert. Instead of manually training an ML model, a data analyst can use Joule to generate SQL code leveraging RPT‑1’s PREDICT function.

Steps include:

  1. Join sales inquiries, historical performance, customer attributes, and other relevant data into a Data Product in SAP Business Data Cloud.
  2. Expose the Data Product to SAP HANA Cloud via zero‑copy.
  3. Use RPT‑1 to instantly generate a new prediction column "sales_conversion_probability"; without training or tuning.

The model derives patterns directly from the historical data and produces high‑quality prediction scores.

You can watch the full demo here (jump to minute 35:23).

 

Available RPT‑1 Versions

SAP RPT‑1 has been generally available since Q4 2025. You can choose from:

  • SAP RPT‑1 Small: optimized for speed and efficiency
  • SAP RPT‑1 Large: optimized for highest accuracy by using more capacity
  • SAP RPT‑1 OSS (Open Source): available on HuggingFace and Github for exploration and learning

Try it yourself!

Don’t have access to a BTP account yet? here is an entry point:

  • Sign up for a 30-day SAP Generative AI Hub trial
  • Follow this step-by-step guide to get started by @sherene_tan 

 

Conclusion

SAP RPT‑1 represents a major shift in how enterprises will deliver predictive insights in the GenAI era. While LLMs excel at language understanding, they are not built for structured, relational business data. RPT‑1 closes this gap and eliminates the complexity of traditional machine learning, drastically reducing cost and compute.

With SAP RPT-1's foundation model purpose‑built for enterprise-grade tabular data, the objective is to enable organizations to operationalize predictive insights faster, more efficiently, and more accurately than ever before.

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: Positioning SAP BTP as part of SAP Business Suite certification
Announcing Our New Course: Positioning SAP CIAM
entry.content:
We are thrilled to introduce our latest course, Positioning SAP CIAM, designed to help professionals understand the strategic value of SAP Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM) and how it drives business success.

Why This Course?

In today’s digital-first world, managing customer identities securely and efficiently is crucial for businesses. SAP CIAM empowers organizations to enhance customer experiences, strengthen security, and accelerate digital transformation. This course is tailored for consultants, business users, and sales representatives who want to master the positioning and business value of SAP CIAM.

Course Objectives

By the end of this course, you will be able to: ✅ Explain the importance of SAP CIAM in modern business environments. ✅ Understand the current market perspective on SAP CIAM and its competitive advantages. ✅ Differentiate between business value drivers and the benefits of SAP CIAM. ✅ Illustrate value creation with SAP CIAM through real-world customer examples.

Course Overview

This course provides a comprehensive introduction to SAP CIAM, covering:

  • The strategic importance of CIAM in digital transformation.
  • How SAP CIAM enhances customer experiences while ensuring security.
  • Market positioning and competitive differentiation.
  • Real-world case studies demonstrating business value.

Whether you're a consultant advising clients, a business user implementing CIAM solutions, or a sales professional positioning SAP CIAM, this course will equip you with the knowledge to articulate its value effectively.

Who Should Enroll?

🔹 Consultants – Learn how to position SAP CIAM in client engagements. 🔹 Business Users– Understand how SAP CIAM improves customer interactions. 🔹 Sales Representatives – Gain insights to effectively sell SAP CIAM solutions.

Learn Today!

Ready to master SAP CIAM positioning? Learn now and take the first step toward becoming an expert in customer identity and access management.

 

We hope you enjoy this course and encourage you to share your valuable feedback with us. Thank you!

entry.title: SAP ICC Introducing New Refreshed Framework: Two Paths to Confidence
entry.content:
SAP always enables partners to extend its solutions in ways that foster innovation while preserving the stability of the core system. This rests on four architectural principles that shape how SAP builds solutions and how partners extend them.

  • Cloud-First architecture
  • Clean Core extensibility principle
  • Business AI and intelligent applications
  • Data-driven business operations

SAP Integration and Certification Center (SAP ICC) goal is maintaining ecosystem quality while avoiding disruptive changes that unnecessarily burden partners or customers.

The certification framework is designed to validate that partner applications enhance our value proposition, maintain the technical qualities required for continuous innovation, and align with our strategic direction and below priorities

  • Platform alignment matters
  • Upgrade resilience is essential
  • Standard integration methods

As a partner, if you are looking to extend SAP solutions, integrate your own solution, build a standalone application from scratch, or develop a comprehensive offering embracing SAP technology, the SAP ICC team provides you with the right services.

Two paths to confidence: Integration Certification and Interoperability Review.

To know more please join us at the

In-person events

Location: SAP Labs India - Whitefield, Bengaluru - 560066

Date: 05th February 2026

SAP Partner Meet-up: Driving Innovations Together

Webinar:

  • EMEA/APAC session
    10:00–11:00 CET (UTC+1) → 14:30 IST, 17:00 SGT, 18:00 CST (China), 20:00 AEST
  • NA/LAC session
    12:00–13:00 ET (UTC‑5) → 11:00 CT (MX), 10:00 MT, 09:00 PT, 14:00 BRT

entry.title: Partner‑Driven Innovation Takes Off with Agent Builder in Joule Studio
entry.content:
In 2025, SAP expanded the Joule ecosystem with Joule Studio, enabling partners to extend Joule through custom skills and autonomous agents. Earlier initiatives demonstrated how partners could build Joule skills to handle targeted workflows and augment enterprise processes, as showcased in the Hack2Build custom Joule skills edition . 

With the Agent Builder now generally available, (read the announcement blog here), SAP has taken a major step forward. Partners can use agentic AI to design custom AI agents capable of planning, reasoning, and executing multi‑step processes across SAP and non‑SAP systems, supported by capabilities such as MCP‑based integrations, multi‑agent orchestration, and business‑context grounding. 

Against this backdrop, the Hack2Build on Custom AI Agents invited partners to explore these capabilities hands‑on for this inaugural edition. 

 

Hack2Build Custom AI Agents Edition 

The Hack2Build Custom AI Agents Edition focused on a select group of early‑adopter partners who received hands‑on coaching from SAP experts to work with the newly released Agent Builder capability. SAP Product and solution architecture specialists supported partners in shaping their designs and aligning them with specific customer needs.

SAP Partners participated in Hack2Build custom AI Agents first edition- January 2026

 Partners overcame early product‑maturity challenges and succeeded in building functional custom AI agents in less than seven days during the hack phase. The initiative culminated in a Live Demo Jam, where all teams presented their working prototypes to a panel of SAP judges.

This was the first Hack2Build where partners worked directly with agentic AI tooling, experimenting with autonomous reasoning, multi‑agent orchestration, system integration patterns, and MCP‑enabled workflows.

🏆Winners

The winning solutions stood out for addressing customer‑critical challenges while thoughtfully complementing SAP’s portfolio. With creative use of agentic AI, the teams demonstrated how autonomous agents can be embedded into real workflows—connecting systems, reasoning over business data, and driving outcomes that matter to customers.

🥇Winner — LTIMindtree: Treasury Auto Ops

Treasury Auto Ops applies agentic AI to transform treasury management with real‑time forecasting, FX exposure assessment, liquidity oversight, and automated hedging recommendations. The agent synthesizes market indicators, macroeconomic signals, and internal treasury data to reach high forecasting accuracy and proactively execute SAP Treasury actions with policy‑compliant governance.
Partners selected for Hack2Build custom AI Agents first edition- January 2026

🥈1st Runner‑Up — Avvale: Early Payment Assistant

Avvale's agent identifies early‑payment discount opportunities across supplier invoices and provides optimized payment recommendations. By balancing liquidity needs with discount potential, it improves working capital, strengthens supplier relationships, and delivers measurable ROI.
Avvale: Early Payment Assistant

 🥉 2nd Runner‑Up — Talan: TALK2BUY

TALK2BUY brings conversational procurement to SAP Cloud ERP. Users can speak or type purchase requisitions in natural language, while the agent enforces procurement policy, recommends compliant items, and sends Microsoft Teams reminders to ensure timely goods‑receipt updates.

Talan: TALK2BUY

 ⭐ Finalists: High‑Quality Solutions from All Participating Partners

  • All for One – Service preparation, escalation & ticket automation agent
  • Capgemini – Smart Vendor Agent to assess supplier data consistency 
  • cbs  – ARIA: Agentic Rate & Inforecord Automation agent
  • DXC Technology – AI Engineering Proposal Assistant (JAEPA)
  • KPMG – Inventory Reconciliation Agent
  • Numen – Financial Performance Advisor
  • NTT Data – FraudCheck Agent
  • Sodales – Job Accommodation & Return‑to‑Work program generator

Special Thanks

A sincere thank you to all participating partners, judges Pavel Penaz, Jan Schneider, Stefan Koenig, Boris Andree, the SAP Build and Joule Studio product teams, and the wider SAP Partner Ecosystem Success units for their support throughout the hack phase.

What’s Next?

The Hack2Build initiative now moves into the BUILD phase, where all participating partners will work over the next 4–5 weeks to realize the full scope of their agentic solutions. Coached by SAP experts, this phase focuses on maturing designs, enriching integrations, and preparing customer‑ready outcomes based on real business requirements and partner domain expertise.

For partners  interested in joining future Hack2Build, registration for the next edition in March is already open.

entry.title: SAP BTP - APIM - Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP): Governing APIs via CPI Provider - Part I
entry.content:
DCRP: Taming Enterprise-Scale SAP Integration API Proxies

Executive Summary

The Problem: Enterprise that contains SAP APIM suffers from "Proxy Sprawl"—where each integration requires a dedicated API proxy, creating a 1:1 relationship that leads to:

  • Deployment bottlenecks (15 min per proxy)
  • Inconsistent security architecture
  • Unacceptable technical debt growth

The Solution: The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) consolidates multiple backend services into a single, domain-based API Proxy using a metadata-driven routing engine (JavaScript + KVM).

Impact:

  • Reduces proxies by up to 96% (100 integrations → 4 proxies)
  • Deployment time: 15 min → 30 seconds -
  • Centralized security, analytics, and traceability -
  • Validated: 4 domains deployed in 8 minutes
My Contribution - Ricardo Viana

Dynamic routing is a native feature in SAP APIM, AWS, and Kong. However, technology alone isn't enough. DCRP provides the governance framework necessary to scale this technology in enterprise environments, including standardized naming conventions, KVM structures, and deployment templates.

Time Investment: 

  • Executive summary: 90 seconds
  • Full technical guide: 25~30 minutes

🎯Target Audience & Prerequisites

Intended for: Integration Architects, Enterprise Architects, Solution Architects, Platform Owners, Governance Teams, and SAP BTP Integration Suite experts with hands-on API Management experience and for anyone passionate about integration — junior, senior, or student level.

Technical Prerequisites:

✅SAP API Management: Proxy design (ProxyEndpoint/TargetEndpoint), Policy execution flow, KeyValueMap configuration
✅JavaScript in APIM: Context variable manipulation, error handling
✅API Proxy Flow Architecture: PreFlow/PostFlow execution order, when  target.url can be modified
✅Enterprise Governance: Naming conventions, change management, security policies

Complexity Level: Advanced.

Not Covered: Basic SAP APIM tutorials, JavaScript fundamentals, beginner proxy creation.

Estimated PoC Time: 2 hours (APIM experts) | 1–2 days (Integration Architects new to APIM)

Introduction

SAP’s official documentation explains how to enable dynamic routing, but it often leaves a gap regarding when, why, and how to govern it at scale. This article aims to bridge that gap by providing a complete framework—from architectural design to hands-on implementation.

Building on my previous post about Advanced Domain Routing, we will now deep-dive into applying the Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) to a real-world scenario: Sales & Distribution (O2C).

The DCRP Value Proposition:

  • Consolidation: A single API Proxy manages multiple sources (Salesforce, Microsoft D365, Vendor CRM).
  • Governance: Full request traceability and standardized security without infrastructure sprawl.
  • Scalability: High-performance routing without the overhead of proxy-level versioning.

What to Expect:
This is Part I of a series dedicated to eliminating integration chaos:

  • Part I (This post): APIM Architecture and DCRP Implementation.
  • Part II: Solving "CPI Package Sprawl" at the orchestration layer.
  • Part III: End-to-end Testing and Monitoring strategy.

Let’s explore the architectural rationale and the step-by-step implementation to apply DCRP in your landscape.

Figure 1 - DCRP - API Proxy Model detailed exploration

1 - The "Sprawl" Problem

In many enterprise landscapes, it is common to find a 1:1 relationship between services and API Proxies. This leads to what we identify as "Proxy Sprawl": an environment where proxies are created for every individual technical service, regardless of their business domain.

Without a central governance authority or standardized naming conventions, these environments quickly become un-governed and unsustainable. As the integration volume grows, the complexity of managing, securing, and monitoring hundreds of individual proxies creates a significant operational bottleneck.

This pattern is not theoretical; DCRP was developed as a direct response to these real-world governance gaps. Following extensive architectural validation, this methodology was formalized to provide a repeatable solution for the SAP Integration Community.

 Figure 2 - The Sprawl Problem

2 - What is DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern) ?

The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) is an architectural design pattern that consolidates multiple backend services into a single, business-domain-oriented API Proxy. Residing at the Gateway Layer, DCRP governs how backend services are exposed and routed without necessitating changes to their underlying protocols (REST or SOAP).

Instead of organizing APIs into technical silos, DCRP aligns them with the organization's business domains. This approach significantly reduces API fragmentation and maintenance overhead while remaining fully compatible with SAP’s API Product offerings.

Figure 3 - The three pillars of DCPR

  • Domain-Centric Organization: - APIs grouped by business domain (Sales, Finance, Logistics) - NOT by technical system (Salesforce, SAP, D365)
  • Metadata-Driven Routing: - KeyValueMap (KVM) stores path → backend mappings - Changes in 30 seconds without redeployment
  • Protocol-Agnostic Gateway: - Supports REST and SOAP backends - No protocol lock-in
2.2 - DCRP Decision Framework

✅Use DCRP When:

  • Domain Density: 10+ APIs in same business domain -
  • High Volatility: Integration changes monthly -
  • Centralized Governance: Critical security requirements -
  • Velocity: Rapid deployment is business-critical **

❌DO NOT Use When:

  • Unrelated services with no business logic connection -
  • Only 1-2 services per domain -
  • Fewer than 10 total APIs -
  • Teams lack governance maturity

Figure 4 - CRP Decision Framework: When to Use vs. When NOT to Use

2.3 - DCRP Architecture Overview: The Power of Dynamic Routing

The diagram below illustrates how the Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) transforms the integration flow. Instead of a fragmented landscape of individual proxies, we consolidate connectivity through a unified, intelligent gateway layer.

The Technical Workflow:

  • Unified Entry Point: Multiple consumer systems—such as Salesforce, Dynamics 365, and Vendor CRMs—connect through a single Sales Domain APIM Proxy.
  • The DCRP Engine: Within the SAP API Management Gateway, the request is processed by the "Brain" of the pattern, which performs a KVM Lookup and executes JavaScript logic to resolve the target backend dynamically.
  • Secure Orchestration: The request is routed via a secure tunnel to the appropriate Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) iFlow.
  • Backend Flexibility: CPI handles the final orchestration with target systems (e.g., S/4HANA, Legacy Systems, or SAP ECC) using protocols like ODATA, SOAP, or HTTPS.
  • Governance at Scale: This model eliminates the need for separate proxies per integration, allowing for centralized monitoring, auditing, and security enforcement through the Analytics Collector and a global Governance Layer.

Figure 5 - DCRP Architecture Overview end-to-end: Three-CRM Integration Scenario

2.4 - The Technical Foundation: KVM + JavaScript

KeyValueMap (KVM) | The "Memory":

  • Stores routing mappings: `httppath:iflowname`
  • Business logic as data,: not hardcoded endpoints
  • Near-instant configuration changes

JavaScript Policy | The "Brain":

  • Queries KVM and validates request paths
  • Dynamically constructs target URLs - Injects governance headers (X-SenderId, X-CorrelationId)
  • Structured error handling (no "silent failures")

Figure 6 - Enginne KVM + JavaScript

2.5 - Proxy Sprawl vs DCRP: The Comparison

The shift from a traditional 1:1 model to a Domain-Centric approach fundamentally changes how an integration landscape scales. The following comparison highlights the efficiency gains of the DCRP methodology:

Real Numbers:

  • 100 integrations across 4 domains - Traditional: 100 proxies × 15 min = 25 hours
  • DCRP: 4 proxies × 2 min = 8 minutes (96% reduction)

Figure 7 - Proxy Sprawl vs DCRP Model

3 - Implementation: Configuring the DCRP API Proxy

This section defines the configuration for the ProxyEndpoint, Routing Rules, and Target Endpoint properties required to deploy a domain-centric architecture.

3.1 - The Entry Contract: One API Proxy per Business Domain

DCRP eliminates proxy sprawl by consolidating multiple technical applications into a single entry contract based on the business domain. This ensures each business area, such as Sales or Finance, has exactly one governed API Proxy.

3.2 - Naming Convention Strategy

A standardized naming convention provides immediate architectural context. DCRP uses the following structure:

[Provider]-[Pattern]-proxy-domain-[BusinessProcess]-[subprocess]

Example:  cpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-o2c

  • cpi: Identifies the provider (SAP Cloud Integration)
  • dcrp: Defines the routing methodology.
  • sales: Identifies the business domain/process (O2C).
  • o2c: sap-subprocess
3.3 - API Proxy Details

The proxy serves as the unified entry point for the domain:

  • Name: cpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-o2c
  • Type: REST
  • Base URL: https://[tenant].apimanagement.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/sales
3.4 - Endpoint,Routing Configuration, Value Mapping and explanation

The Routing Rules act as a gatekeeper, ensuring only authorized paths reach the dynamic engine in this sample we are working only with orders process.

Proxy Endpoint (Proxy Endpoint):

  • Route Rule Name: orders
  • Condition: proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**"

Figure 8 - Configuring the Route Rule Condition

Target Endpoint Properties:

These properties provide the base connectivity for the routing engine:

  • Property Value
    • cpi.host https://[tenant].cfapps.eu20.hana.ondemand.com
    • cpi.basepath /http/orders

Figure 9 - The main properties for the Java Script create the SAP CPI endpoint

Configuration of Key Value Mapping (KVM)

Following the suggested naming convention, you must create one Key-Value Mapping for each SAP CPI package:

  • Example Maps: cpipackage-nx.procurement.domain.process, cpipackage-nx.sales.domain.process, etc.

Figure 10 - API-Proxy vs Key Value Mapping

KVM Structure: Each map contains three primary entries:

  • packagename: The technical CPI package name (e.g., nx.sales.domain.process).
  • sapprocess: The business domain description (e.g., SAP SD & O2C - Sales Process).
  • iflowname: The routing metadata string.

Figure 11 - Key Value Mapping Configuration

Understanding the iflowname String

The iflowname entry is the "routing table" for the DCRP. It contains pairs consisting of the Semantic Path Suffix (used by the consumer) and the Technical iFlow Name (the target in CPI). This string is parsed by the JavaScript "Brain," so maintaining the pattern is critical.

Example Configuration: iflowname: inbound:SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Inbound,tracking:SO.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.

The java script support almost all types of demiliters , but you can't use two dots (:) because this is the separation of http path url from SAP CPI and the Iflow name, between the records you can use any my suggestion is ( , ) 

// Auto-detect delimiter used in KVM string
var supportedDelimiters = [',', ';', '|', '/', '\\', '[', ']', '(', ')', '-', '_'];

What does this configuration mean?

It maps the public HTTP URL suffix to the specific CPI technical endpoint:

  • inbound $\rightarrow$ Routes to SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Inbound.
  • tracking $\rightarrow$ Routes to SO.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.

inbound:SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Process.Inbound

  • cpi endpoint: /orders/inbound

tracking:SO.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.Provider
  • cpi endpoint: /orders/tracking
The SAP CPI Packages:

Following the DCRP methodology, SAP CPI packages are organized as domain-specific containers that house multiple related integration flows.

 The Full Configuration Flow

  • Condition: The Proxy Endpoint validates the path (e.g., proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**").
  • Lookup: The "Brain" extracts the iflowname string from the KVM.
  • Parsing: The JavaScript splits the string using the , delimiter to extract individual pairs.
  • Generation: The engine matches the incoming suffix (e.g., /inbound) and generates the final CPI Target URL.
  • End url: https://<cpi>/orders/inbound
function resolveAdrpRouting() {
    var kvmMapIflowString = context.getVariable("iflowname");

if (kvmMapIflowString) {
        var routes = kvmMapIflowString.split(","); 
        for (var i = 0; i < routes.length; i++) {
            var parts = routes[i].split(":"); 
            if (parts.length >= 2) {
                var pathKey = parts[0].trim();   
                if (rawSuffix.indexOf("/" + pathKey) !== -1) {
                    resourceName = "/" + pathKey; // Define "/inbound"
                    iflowName = parts[1].trim();
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    }

Note: This metadata-driven approach allows you to add new integrations in 30 seconds just by updating the KVM string—no proxy redeployment required.

3.5 - The Semantic Facade: One Proxy, Multiple Intentions

Instead of deploying separate proxies, all consumers use the SAME API Proxy. Differentiation handled via path suffixes.

Consumer View (Public API):

  • POST https://apim/sales/orders/inbound
  • POST https://apim/sales/orders/tracking

Backend Reality (CPI): 

  • https://cpi/http/orders/SO.01.Salesforce.Order.Inbound 
  • https://cpi/http/orders/SO.02.Tracking.Provider

Key Benefits:

  • Total Abstraction: Consumer contract unchanged if backend migrates
  • End-to-End Monitoring: Unified analytics for entire Sales domain
  • Centralized Security: One OAuth2 policy for all
  • Unified Traffic Control: Spike Arrest and Quotas by domain

 Figure 12 - Public API paths mapped to technical CPI endpoints

3.6 - The Routing Engine: Execution Flow

Every request passes through three stages:

Stage 1: Proxy Endpoint Pre-Flow

  • Security policies (OAuth2, API Key)
  • Request validation (payload size, content type)
  • Traffic control (Spike Arrest, Quotas)
  • ❌Immediate rejection if policies fail

Stage 2: Routing Rules

  • Evaluate: proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**"
  • ✅Match → forward to Target Endpoint
  • ❌No match → 404 error

Stage 3: Target Endpoint Pre-Flow

  • DCRP Brain executes here (last point to modify target.url)
  • KVM Lookup → JavaScript Routing → URL Construction

Figure 13 - Three-stage request processing with DCRP logic in Target Pre-Flow

3.7 - The DCRP Brain: Policy Stages

The routing engine executes through four policy stages Target Endpoint - PRE-Flow:

  • Key Value Mapping: Retrieves httppath, iflowname, and sapprocess.
  • JavaScript Routing: Performs variable verification, validation, and injection of headers.
  • RaiseFaultException: Blocks the process in case of exceptions, missing variables, or KVM misconfigurations.
  • Statistics Collection: Captures custom dimension variables for the SAP APIM Dashboard.

The Brain Components: Key Value Mapping and JavaScript Logic

The DCRP pattern transforms the gateway into a high-scale, programmable router by decoupling configuration from execution and moving routing logic into metadata.

Figure 14 - KVM + Java Script

3.8 Scaling Model: Vertical vs. Horizontal

Vertical Scaling (30 Seconds): Add new iFlows to an existing domain by updating the KVM string. No infrastructure changes are required.

Horizontal Scaling (2 Minutes): Onboard an entirely new business domain (e.g., Finance) by cloning the domain template and updating only the mapIdentifier.

Figure 15 - Two-Level scaling strategy

Real-World Validation: 4 Business Domains Deployed

The methodology was validated by deploying four business domains—Sales, Logistics, Finance, and Procurement—managing 8 total iFlows.

Validation Metrics:

API Proxies Deployed: 4.

  • JavaScript Policies Reused: 1 universal routing engine.
    Total Deployment Time: Approximately 8 minutes (~2 minutes per domain).
  • Resilience: While DCRP returns an HTTP 404 if a specific iFlow endpoint is missing, the routing logic itself remains correct and operational.
  • Business Domain API Proxy CPI Package iFlows Time

Business Domain API Proxy CPI Package iFlows Time

Sales & O2Ccpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-o2ccpipackage-nx.sales.domain.process22 min
Logistics & Shippingcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-logistics-scmcpipackage-nx.logistics.domain.process22 min
Finance & Billingcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-finance-r2rcpipackage-nx.finance.domain.process22 min
Procurementcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-procurement-s2pcpipackage-nx.procurement.domain.process22 min
  • Results:
  • 4 API Proxies managing 8 iFlows
  • JavaScript policies reused: 1 (universal routing engine)
  • Total deployment time: ~8 minutes

Total Time = (N domains × 2 min) + (M iFlows × 30 sec)

3.9 - The JavaScript "Brain" (Complete Code)

Figure 16 - Java Script coding

The code of java script is into spoiler below:

Spoiler
/**
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 * 
 *   THE DCRP ROUTING ENGINE
 * 
 *   MISSION: Transform static gateway infrastructure into intelligent,
 *            metadata-driven routing orchestration at enterprise scale.
 * 
 *   CAPABILITIES:
 *   ✓ Dynamic iFlow resolution via Key-Value Map metadata
 *   ✓ Path parameter preservation (single or multi-level IDs)
 *   ✓ Query string integrity across gateway boundaries
 *   ✓ Governance header injection (X-SenderId, X-CorrelationId)
 *   ✓ Analytics variable collection for observability
 *   ✓ Zero code deployment for new integrations (metadata-only scaling)
 * 
 *   AUTHOR: Viana - SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
 *   PATTERN: Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP)
 *   VERSION: 1.0 - Production Ready
 * 
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 */
function resolveAdrpRouting() {
    try {
        var cpiHost = context.getVariable("target.cpi.host");
        var cpiBasepathRaw = context.getVariable("target.cpi.basepath");
        var iflowname = context.getVariable("iflowname");
        var pathSuffix = context.getVariable("proxy.pathsuffix");
        var senderId = context.getVariable("request.header.X-SenderId");
        var correlationId = context.getVariable("request.header.X-CorrelationId");
        var queryParams = context.getVariable("request.querystring") || "";

        if (!cpiHost || !cpiBasepathRaw || !iflowname || !pathSuffix) {
            context.setVariable("routing.failed", true);
            context.setVariable("routing.error", "MISSING_REQUIRED_VARIABLES");
            throw new Error("DCRP: Missing required variables for routing");
        }

        // ========================================
        // SECTION 1.5: EXTRACT PATH SEGMENTS
        // ========================================

        var cleanPathSuffix = pathSuffix.replace(/^\/+/, '');
        var segments = cleanPathSuffix.split('/');
        var firstSegment = segments[0]; // "orders" ou "fodase"
        var remainingPath = segments.slice(1).join('/'); // "inbound"

        // Build dynamic basepath: /http/{firstSegment}
        var cpiBasepath = "/http/" + firstSegment;

        // ========================================
        // SECTION 2: FLEXIBLE DELIMITER DETECTION
        // ========================================

        var supportedDelimiters = [',', ';', '|', '/', '\\', '[', ']', '(', ')', '-', '_'];
        var delimiter = null;

        for (var i = 0; i < supportedDelimiters.length; i++) {
            if (iflowname.indexOf(supportedDelimiters[i]) !== -1) {
                delimiter = supportedDelimiters[i];
                break;
            }
        }

        if (!delimiter) {
            context.setVariable("routing.failed", true);
            context.setVariable("routing.error", "NO_DELIMITER_FOUND");
            throw new Error("DCRP: No valid delimiter found in KVM value");
        }

        // ========================================
        // SECTION 3: KVM PARSING & PATH MATCHING
        // ========================================

        var entries = iflowname.split(delimiter);
        var routes = [];

        for (var j = 0; j < entries.length; j++) {
            var entry = entries[j].trim();
            if (entry.length === 0) continue;

            var parts = entry.split(':');
            if (parts.length !== 2) {
                context.setVariable("routing.failed", true);
                context.setVariable("routing.error", "INVALID_KVM_FORMAT");
                throw new Error("DCRP: Invalid KVM format - expected 'path:iflowname', got: " + entry);
            }

            routes.push({
                path: parts[0].trim(),
                iflow: parts[1].trim()
            });
        }

        routes.sort(function(a, b) {
            return b.path.length - a.path.length;
        });

        var matchedRoute = null;

        for (var k = 0; k < routes.length; k++) {
            var routePath = routes[k].path.replace(/^\/+/, '');

            if (remainingPath === routePath || remainingPath.indexOf(routePath + '/') === 0) {
                matchedRoute = routes[k];
                break;
            }
        }

        if (!matchedRoute) {
            context.setVariable("routing.failed", true);
            context.setVariable("routing.error", "ROUTE_NOT_FOUND");
            context.setVariable("routing.pathsuffix", pathSuffix);
            throw new Error("DCRP: No matching route found for path: " + pathSuffix);
        }

        // ========================================
        // SECTION 4: GOVERNANCE HEADER INJECTION
        // ========================================

        if (senderId) {
            context.setVariable("request.header.X-SenderId", senderId);
        }

        if (correlationId) {
            context.setVariable("request.header.X-CorrelationId", correlationId);
        }

        // ========================================
        // SECTION 5: DYNAMIC URL CONSTRUCTION
        // ========================================

        var targetUrl = cpiHost + cpiBasepath + "/" + matchedRoute.path;

        if (queryParams && queryParams.length > 0) {
            targetUrl += "?" + queryParams;
        }

        context.setVariable("target.url", targetUrl);
        context.setVariable("target.copy.pathsuffix", false);
        context.setVariable("target.copy.queryparams", false);

        // ========================================
        // SUCCESS: SET ROUTING METADATA
        // ========================================

        context.setVariable("routing.success", true);
        context.setVariable("routing.matched.path", matchedRoute.path);
        context.setVariable("routing.matched.iflow", matchedRoute.iflow);
        context.setVariable("routing.target.url", targetUrl);
        context.setVariable("routing.selected.basepath", cpiBasepath);

        context.setVariable("idiflow", matchedRoute.iflow);
        context.setVariable("idcorrelation", context.getVariable("request.header.X-CorrelationId"));
        context.setVariable("idsender", senderId || "UNKNOWN");

    } catch (error) {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", true);
        context.setVariable("routing.exception", error.message || error.toString());

        print("DCRP Routing Error: " + error.message);
        print("PathSuffix: " + pathSuffix);
        print("KVM Value: " + iflowname);
    }
}

Add in seguency in the Pre-Flow TargetEndpoint - Raise Fault Routing:

condition: routing.failed = "true"

Figure 17 - Raise Fault in case of failure in the "Brain Java Script Routing"

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?>
<RaiseFault async="false" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <FaultResponse>
        <Set>
            <StatusCode>404</StatusCode>
            <ReasonPhrase>Route Not Found</ReasonPhrase>
        </Set>
        <Set>
            <Payload contentType="application/json">{
  "error": "ROUTE_NOT_FOUND",
  "message": "{routing.error.message}",
  "details": {
    "error_code": "{routing.error}",
    "blocked_segment": "{routing.blocked.segment}",
    "blocked_path": "{routing.blocked.path}",
    "available_paths": "{routing.available.paths}",
    "timestamp": "{system.timestamp}",
    "correlation_id": "{request.header.X-CorrelationId}"
  }
}</Payload>
        </Set>
        <Add>
            <Headers>
                <Header name="Content-Type">application/json</Header>
                <Header name="X-Routing-Error">{routing.error}</Header>
