                <Header name="X-Correlation-Id">{request.header.X-CorrelationId}</Header>
            </Headers>
        </Add>
    </FaultResponse>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>true</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
</RaiseFault>

4 - Operational Simulation and Architectural Flow

This section demonstrates the practical execution of the DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern) framework by simulating how the routing engine processes a request through the landscape.

Architectural Flow Simulation

Based on the provided technical diagram, the process follows a structured path from the initiating system to the final integration endpoint:

  • Request Initiation: External systems such as
    • Salesforce -  http://<apim>/sales/orders/inbound
    • Dynamics 365 - http://<apim>/sales/orders/status
    • Vendor CRMs  - http://<apim>/sales/orders/tracking
  • The DCRP Engine: Upon reaching the SAP API Management Gateway, the DCRP Enginne is triggered to manage the transaction logic.
    • Metadata Retrieval (KVM Lookup): 
      • Inbound:iflowname | sapprocess | cpipackage
      • Tracking:iflowname | sapprocess | cpipackage
      • Status: iflowname | sapprocess | cpipackage
    • Logical Resolution (JavaScript): The JavaScript component processes the proxy.pathsuffix against the metadata retrieved from the KVM to determine the correct technical path.
      • http;//<cpi>/orders/inbound
      • http;//<cpi>/orders/tracking
      • http://<cpi>/orders/status
      • Header details
    • Dynamic Routing: The engine resolves the target and dynamically routes the traffic to the corresponding SAP BTP Integration Suite iFlow.

Architect's Note: This simulation highlights the decoupling of the consumer from the technical backend. The source systems interact only with a standardized semantic layer, while the routing engine manages all technical complexities internally.

Figure 18 - Clear orientation how works the solution from the sender to SAP APIM.

5 - Scaling Strategies: Manual vs Dynamic Routing

Scaling DCRP effectively requires choosing between two distinct deployment approaches, depending on the complexity and regional requirements of the enterprise.

Two Strategic Approaches:

Approach 1: Single Global Template:
  • How it works: Create 1 Routing Rule per path (`/orders/**`, `/customers/**`)
  • Configuration: - 1 proxy per domain - N routing rules (1 per path) -
  • Fixed:`cpi.basepath` per rule
  • Time per new path: ~2 minutes (add routing rule + KVM entry)
  • Best for: Small domains (<5 paths), stable environments

Figure 19 - Template part 1

Figure 20 - Template part 2

Most importantly, you must ensure the Target Endpoint PRE-Flow is ready with the DCRP logic. While this approach provides explicit routing visibility, any additional requirements—such as specific security methods, quotas, or spike control—must be adjusted accordingly.

Required Policies (Target Endpoint PreFlow):

  • Key Value Mapping
  • JavaScript Routing
  • Raise Fault
  • Statistics Collector

Once this setup is finalized, it takes approximately 2 to 3 minutes to have a fully functional API Proxy ready for operation, and less than 30 seconds to update metadata in the Key Value Mapping.

Figure 21 - Pre-Flow setup - ADRP brain

Approach 2: Dynamic Multi-Path Routing (RECOMMENDED)

How it works:

  • Single catch-all route (/**)
  • JavaScript extracts first segment dynamically
  • cpi.basepath is comma-delimited string of all paths

Configuration:

  • 1 proxy per domain
  • 1 catch-all routing rule
  • Security Shield validates domain whitelist

Time per new path: ~30 seconds (update cpi.basepath string + KVM + Security whitelist)

Best for: Enterprise scale, frequent changes

Example Configuration

Target Endpoint Property: cpi.basepath

Initial Value:

/http/orders,/http/customers,/http/invoices,/http/prices

Adding new path (payments):

/http/orders,/http/customers,/http/invoices,/http/prices,/http/payments

Figure 22 - Scalation mode hard

Architect's Note Dynamic Multi-Path Routing: This simulation highlights the decoupling of the consumer from the technical backend. The source systems interact only with a standardized semantic layer, while the routing engine manages all technical complexities internally.

Figure 23 - Clear orientation how works the solution with Dynamic Multi-Path Routing from the sender to SAP APIM.

Security Shield: Critical for Approach 2

Because Approach 2 uses a catch-all route (/**), every request reaches the routing engine. The Security Shield blocks malicious requests before they consume resources.

When adding new paths, you MUST update 3 configurations:

  • Target Endpoint Property (cpi.basepath):
/http/orders,/http/customers,/http/payments ← ADD HERE
  • Security Whitelist (JS-Security-Shield.js, Section 2)
var allowedDomains = [
    "sales", "orders", "customers", "invoices", 
    "payments"  // ← ADD HERE
];
  • KVM Entry (iflowname):
orders:SO.01...,customers:SO.02...,payments:SO.03...  ← ADD HERE

Execution Order (Proxy PreFlow):

  1. JS-Security-Shield → Validates domain whitelist, blocks attacks
  2. RF-SecurityThreat → Returns 403 if threat detected
  3. Quote Limiting → Quota Enforcement
  4. Spike Arrets → Spike Arrest
  5. KVM Lookup → Only runs if security passed

Total Time: 30 seconds (10s each)

Figure 24 - Setup of cpi.basepath variable in the target endpoint with full string or new incomming process.

To do this setup this takes me 1 minutes.

5.2 - How Dynamic Routing Works

Request Flow Example

Request Path First Segment Dynamic Basepath KVM Match Final CPI URL
/sales/customerscustomers/http/customersSO.01.Customer.MasterDatahttps://cpi/http/customers
/sales/ordersorders/http/ordersSO.02.Order.Processhttps://cpi/http/orders
/sales/invoicesinvoices/http/invoicesSO.03.Invoice.Billinghttps://cpi/http/invoices
 
Key: The JavaScript extracts the first segment (customers, orders) and builds the basepath dynamically: /http/{firstSegment}

Why Security Shield is Critical for Dynamic Routing ?

When transitioning from explicit routing rules (/orders/**, /customers/**) to a catch-all route (/**), the security attack surface fundamentally changes. The Risk: Open Gateway Vulnerability

  • EVERY path reaches the routing engine
  • ⚠️No implicit protection at routing layer
  • ⚠️Attack surface = entire gateway

Real-World Attack Scenarios

Without the Security Shield, malicious requests bypass gateway protection:

Attack Type Example Request Without Shield With Shield
Path Traversal/sales/../admin/delete✅Reaches KVM/JS❌Blocked at PreFlow (403)
SQL Injection/orders?id=1 OR 1=1✅Reaches KVM/JS❌Blocked at PreFlow (403)
Domain Hijacking/hacker-domain/exploit✅Reaches KVM/JS❌Blocked at PreFlow (403)
Command Injection/orders?cmd=;cat /etc/passwd✅Reaches KVM/JS❌Blocked at PreFlow (403)

The Security Architecture Decision

Question: "Why not just rely on KVM routing to return 404 for invalid paths?"

Answer: Defense-in-depth principle.

  1. Resource Efficiency: Blocking at PreFlow saves CPU cycles on KVM lookup + JavaScript execution
  2. Attack Intelligence: Security Shield logs threat patterns for SIEM/analytics
  3. Blast Radius: A KVM misconfiguration (e.g., wrong delimiter) could expose all paths
  4. Audit Compliance: SOC 2 / ISO 27001 require explicit security validation at gateway layer

Performance Impact

From production telemetry (4 domains, 30 days):

Scenario Time Spent Decision
Valid request (Security passed)+10ms overhead✅Acceptable (8% of total)
Blocked attack (Security rejected)10ms (no KVM/JS)✅ Saves 100ms

Key Insight: Security Shield pays for itself by preventing resource waste on malicious requests.

Java Script Thread Hacking protection - ( Complete Code )

Figure 25 - Java Script Policy - Hacking thread protection Domain-Specific Templates

In this case you need to add this java script in the Proxy End-point - Pre-Flow to stop imidiatly any hacking or sql injection, because now the routing mechanism is not based on path, is start - proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/**"

Figure 23 - Java Script Thread Protection - ProxyEndpoint - Preflow

Add in seguency - RaiseFault Hacking thead:

condition: security.raise.fault = "true"

Figure 24 - Raise Fault Thread of Hacking, sql injection and others.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?>
<RaiseFault async="false" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <FaultResponse>
        <Set>
            <StatusCode>403</StatusCode>
            <ReasonPhrase>Forbidden - Security Threat Detected</ReasonPhrase>
        </Set>
        <Set>
            <Payload contentType="application/json">{
                  "error": "SECURITY_THREAT_DETECTED",
                  "message": "Request blocked by security policies",
                  "details": {
                    "threat_type": "{security.threat.type}",
                    "blocked_path": "{security.blocked.path}",
                    "client_ip": "{client.ip}",
                    "user_agent": "{request.header.User-Agent}",
                    "timestamp": "{system.timestamp}",
                    "correlation_id": "{request.header.X-CorrelationId}",
                    "request_id": "{messageid}"
                  }
                }
            </Payload>
        </Set>
        <Add>
            <Headers>
                <Header name="Content-Type">application/json</Header>
                <Header name="X-Security-Status">BLOCKED</Header>
                <Header name="X-Threat-Type">{security.threat.type}</Header>
                <Header name="X-Correlation-Id">{request.header.X-CorrelationId}</Header>
            </Headers>
        </Add>
    </FaultResponse>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>true</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
</RaiseFault>

The code of java script is into spoiler below:

Spoiler
/**
 * ============================================
 * DCRP SECURITY SHIELD v1.0
 * ============================================
 * PURPOSE: Protect /** catch-all route from malicious requests
 * AUTHOR: Ricardo Viana
 * RUNS: BEFORE JS-Routing in Proxy PreFlow
 * 
 * FEATURES:
 * - Path Traversal Protection
 * - SQL Injection Detection
 * - XSS Protection
 * - Domain Whitelist Enforcement
 * - Header Validation
 * - Bot Protection
 * ============================================
 */

try {
    // ============================================
    // CONTEXT EXTRACTION
    // ============================================
    var pathSuffix = context.getVariable("proxy.pathsuffix") || "";
    var queryString = context.getVariable("request.querystring") || "";
    var userAgent = context.getVariable("request.header.User-Agent") || "";
    var contentType = context.getVariable("request.header.Content-Type") || "";
    var method = context.getVariable("request.verb") || "";
    var clientIp = context.getVariable("client.ip") || "";

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 1: PATH TRAVERSAL PROTECTION
    // ============================================
    var pathTraversalPatterns = [
        /\.\./,                 // ../
        /%2e%2e/i,              // URL-encoded ..
        /%252e/i,               // Double URL-encoded .
        /\.\\/,                 // .\ (Windows path)
        /%5c/i,                 // URL-encoded backslash
        /\/\//,                 // Double slashes
        /etc\/passwd/i,         // Linux system files
        /windows\/system/i,     // Windows system paths
        /proc\/self/i,          // Linux proc filesystem
        /\.\.%2f/i,             // Mixed encoding
        /\.\.;/i                // Semicolon bypass attempt
    ];

    for (var i = 0; i < pathTraversalPatterns.length; i++) {
        if (pathTraversalPatterns[i].test(pathSuffix)) {
            context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "PATH_TRAVERSAL");
            context.setVariable("security.threat.pattern", pathTraversalPatterns[i].toString());
            context.setVariable("security.threat.path", pathSuffix);
            context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
            throw new Error("SECURITY: Path Traversal attempt detected");
        }
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 2: DOMAIN WHITELIST VALIDATION
    // ============================================
    var allowedDomains = [
        "sales", "orders", "customers", "invoices", "prices", "quotations",
        "finance", "accounts", "journals", "payments", "reconciliations",
        "reports", "closings", "taxes", "budgets",
        "logistics", "shipments", "inventory", "deliveries", "warehouses",
        "tracking", "carriers", "returns", "pickpack",
        "procurement", "requisitions", "suppliers", "contracts",
        "buyers", "approvals", "receipts", "catalogs"
    ];

    var segments = pathSuffix.split("/").filter(function(s) {
        return s.length > 0;
    });
    var firstSegment = segments.length > 0 ? segments[0].toLowerCase() : "";

    var isDomainAllowed = false;
    for (var j = 0; j < allowedDomains.length; j++) {
        if (firstSegment === allowedDomains[j]) {
            isDomainAllowed = true;
            break;
        }
    }

    if (!isDomainAllowed && firstSegment !== "") {
        context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "INVALID_DOMAIN");
        context.setVariable("security.threat.path", pathSuffix);
        context.setVariable("security.threat.segment", firstSegment);
        context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
        throw new Error("SECURITY: Invalid domain '" + firstSegment + "' not in whitelist");
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 3: SQL INJECTION DETECTION
    // ============================================
    var sqlPatterns = [
        /(\bOR\b|\bAND\b)\s+[\w\d]+\s*=\s*[\w\d]+/i,
        /UNION\s+(ALL\s+)?SELECT/i,
        /DROP\s+(TABLE|DATABASE)/i,
        /INSERT\s+INTO/i,
        /DELETE\s+FROM/i,
        /UPDATE\s+\w+\s+SET/i,
        /--/,
        /\/\*/,
        /;\s*DROP/i,
        /xp_cmdshell/i,
        /exec(\s|\+)+(s|x)p\w+/i
    ];

    var fullUrl = pathSuffix + (queryString ? "?" + queryString : "");

    for (var k = 0; k < sqlPatterns.length; k++) {
        if (sqlPatterns[k].test(fullUrl)) {
            context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "SQL_INJECTION");
            context.setVariable("security.threat.pattern", sqlPatterns[k].toString());
            context.setVariable("security.threat.url", fullUrl);
            context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
            throw new Error("SECURITY: SQL Injection attempt detected");
        }
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 4: XSS PROTECTION
    // ============================================
    var xssPatterns = [
        /<script[\s\S]*?>/i,
        /javascript:/i,
        /onerror\s*=/i,
        /onload\s*=/i,
        /onclick\s*=/i,
        /<iframe[\s\S]*?>/i,
        /<embed[\s\S]*?>/i,
        /<object[\s\S]*?>/i,
        /eval\s*\(/i,
        /expression\s*\(/i,
        /vbscript:/i,
        /<img[\s\S]*?onerror/i
    ];

    for (var m = 0; m < xssPatterns.length; m++) {
        if (xssPatterns[m].test(fullUrl)) {
            context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "XSS_ATTEMPT");
            context.setVariable("security.threat.pattern", xssPatterns[m].toString());
            context.setVariable("security.threat.url", fullUrl);
            context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
            throw new Error("SECURITY: XSS attempt detected");
        }
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 5: COMMAND INJECTION PROTECTION
    // ============================================
    var commandInjectionPatterns = [
        /;\s*cat\s+/i,
        /\|\s*ls\s+/i,
        /`.*`/,
        /\$\(.*\)/,
        /&&/,
        /\|\|/,
        />\s*\/dev\/null/i,
        /curl\s+/i,
        /wget\s+/i,
        /nc\s+/i
    ];

    for (var n = 0; n < commandInjectionPatterns.length; n++) {
        if (commandInjectionPatterns[n].test(fullUrl)) {
            context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "COMMAND_INJECTION");
            context.setVariable("security.threat.pattern", commandInjectionPatterns[n].toString());
            context.setVariable("security.threat.url", fullUrl);
            context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
            throw new Error("SECURITY: Command Injection attempt detected");
        }
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 6: HEADER VALIDATION
    // ============================================
    if (!userAgent || userAgent.length < 5) {
        context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "MISSING_USER_AGENT");
        context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
        throw new Error("SECURITY: Invalid or missing User-Agent header");
    }

    if ((method === "POST" || method === "PUT")) {
        if (!contentType || contentType.length === 0) {
            context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "MISSING_CONTENT_TYPE");
            context.setVariable("security.threat.method", method);
            context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
            throw new Error("SECURITY: Missing Content-Type header for " + method + " request");
        }
    }

    var suspiciousUAPatterns = [
        /sqlmap/i, /nikto/i, /nmap/i, /masscan/i, /acunetix/i,
        /nessus/i, /burpsuite/i, /metasploit/i, /havij/i
    ];

    for (var p = 0; p < suspiciousUAPatterns.length; p++) {
        if (suspiciousUAPatterns[p].test(userAgent)) {
            context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "SUSPICIOUS_USER_AGENT");
            context.setVariable("security.threat.user_agent", userAgent);
            context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
            throw new Error("SECURITY: Suspicious User-Agent detected");
        }
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 7: PATH LENGTH VALIDATION
    // ============================================
    var maxPathLength = 2048;

    if (pathSuffix.length > maxPathLength) {
        context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "PATH_TOO_LONG");
        context.setVariable("security.threat.path_length", pathSuffix.length.toString());
        context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
        throw new Error("SECURITY: Path length exceeds maximum allowed (" + maxPathLength + " chars)");
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 8: SUSPICIOUS PATTERNS
    // ============================================
    var suspiciousPatterns = [
        /admin/i, /config/i, /\.xml$/i, /\.json$/i, /\.env$/i,
        /\.git/i, /\.svn/i, /backup/i, /phpinfo/i, /test/i, /debug/i
    ];

    for (var q = 0; q < suspiciousPatterns.length; q++) {
        if (suspiciousPatterns[q].test(pathSuffix)) {
            context.setVariable("security.threat.detected", "SUSPICIOUS_PATH");
            context.setVariable("security.threat.pattern", suspiciousPatterns[q].toString());
            context.setVariable("security.threat.path", pathSuffix);
            context.setVariable("security.threat.ip", clientIp);
            throw new Error("SECURITY: Suspicious path pattern detected");
        }
    }

    // ============================================
    // SECTION 9: SUCCESS
    // ============================================
    context.setVariable("security.validated", "true");
    context.setVariable("security.validated.timestamp", Date.now().toString());
    context.setVariable("security.status", "PASSED");
    context.setVariable("security.domain", firstSegment);
    context.setVariable("custom.security.status", "PASSED");
    context.setVariable("custom.security.domain", firstSegment);

} catch (error) {
    // ============================================
    // SECURITY THREAT DETECTED
    // ============================================
    context.setVariable("security.failed", "true");
    context.setVariable("security.error", error.message);
    context.setVariable("security.raise.fault", "true");
    context.setVariable("custom.security.status", "BLOCKED");
    context.setVariable("custom.security.threat",
        context.getVariable("security.threat.detected") || "UNKNOWN");
    context.setVariable("custom.security.path", pathSuffix);
    context.setVariable("custom.security.ip", clientIp);
    context.setVariable("custom.security.method", method);
    context.setVariable("custom.security.user_agent", userAgent);
}
When to Use Each Approach

Use Approach 1 (Manual) when:

  • Domain has <5 paths and rarely changes
  • Team prefers explicit routing rules for visibility
  • Legacy environment with existing path-based architecture

Use Approach 2 (Dynamic) when:

  • Domain has >5 paths OR frequent changes expected
  • Enterprise scale with multiple regional deployments
  • Need maximum deployment velocity (30s per new path)
Comparison Table
Feature Approach 1 (Manual) Approach 2 (Dynamic)
Routing RuleN rules (1 per path)1 catch-all (/**)
cpi.basepathFixed per ruleComma-delimited string
JavaScriptMatches KVM onlyExtracts segment + matches KVM
SecurityRule-basedWhitelist validation required
Time per new path~2 min (add rule)~30 sec (update string)
Best for<5 paths, stableEnterprise, high-change
ROI Calculation (Approach 2)

Scaling 4 domains with 8 paths each:

Approach 1 (Manual):
4 domains × 8 paths × 2 min = 64 minutes

Approach 2 (Dynamic):
4 domains × 1 setup (2 min) + 32 paths × 30 sec = 8 min + 16 min = 24 minutes

Savings: 40 minutes (62% faster)

Figure 26 - Scaling Strategies: Manual vs Dynamic Multi-Path Routing

6 - API Contracts, Versioning & Openapi Management

Why DCRP Proxies Don't Need Versioning

In traditional API management, versioning is a primary driver of Infrastructure Sprawl. Every new version typically spawns a new proxy (e.g., v1-api, v2-api), forcing architects to manage multiple redundant infrastructures.

DCRP eliminates this proliferation by recognizing that the proxy is a stateless router with an immutable base path, not the owner of the API contract.

Fixed Entry Point:

      • The proxy base path remains constant (e.g., /sales/orders/**).
      • Versioned Routing: API versions live in the path suffix (e.g., /v1/, /v2/).
      • Decoupled Logic: These versions are mapped to specific iFlows via KVM metadata, keeping the proxy itself singular and unchanged.

 Figure 27 - Contracts & Governance

Step Traditional 1:1 Proxy Model DCRP Model

      • Action Create new proxy, configure security, routing, and targets, then redeploy.
      • Deploy new iFlow and update a single KVM metadata entry.
      • Time Effort ~15 minutes ~30 seconds
      • Outcome Managing 4 separate proxy infrastructures. Still managing 1 proxy infrastructure.

Client Interaction Examples:

      • Legacy: POST /sales/orders/v1/inbound → Routes to v1 iFlow.
      • Current: POST /sales/orders/v2/inbound → Routes to v2 iFlow.
      • New: POST /sales/orders/v4/inbound → Routes to v4 iFlow.
      • Latest (Default): POST /sales/orders/inbound → Dynamically routes to v4 via KVM.

The proxy base path /sales/orders/** never changed., providing a stable, governed interface for consumers regardless of backend evolution.

6.1 - OpenAPI Management: The Hybrid Approach

In the DCRP model, API contracts (OpenAPI specifications) are decoupled from both the proxy and the iFlow, residing in a central API Portal or repository. This hybrid model ensures a clean separation of concerns:

KVM: Stores runtime routing metadata (path → endpoint).

OpenAPI: Stores design-time contract metadata (request/response schemas).

API Portal: Provides discovery, documentation, and governance.

The Execution Flow:
When a developer needs to integrate, they download the spec (e.g., sales-orders-v2.yaml) from the Portal and generate their client code. When they call the DCRP proxy at /sales/orders/v2, the proxy consults the KVM and routes the request to the corresponding v2 iFlow.

Figure 28 - Template prespective

6.2 - KVM vs OpenAPI: Complementary, Not Redundant

At first glance, KVM and OpenAPI might seem to overlap. Both describe APIs. But they serve distinct purposes:

KVM answers: "Where should I route this request?"

Input: /sales/orders/v2
Output: 
OpenAPI answers: "What schema should this request/response have?"

Input: POST /sales/orders/v2
Output: Request body must include {orderId, customerId, items[]}, returns {status, orderNumber}

Both are necessary for complete governance:

      • Without KVM: No dynamic routing (back to static proxy-per-integration)
      • Without OpenAPI: No contract validation (clients and servers drift out of sync)

DCRP uses KVM for runtime routing decisions and OpenAPI for design-time contract definition and optional runtime validation.

Figure 29 - Complementary - DCRP and OpenAPI

7 - Statistics Collector – Capturing Business Intelligence

While the JavaScript "Brain" handles routing, the Statistics Collector provides the visibility necessary for production management. This policy captures technical metadata and transforms it into actionable business intelligence within the SAP API Management analytics database.
Purpose: Records routing metadata such as iFlow name, package name, domain process, and tracking IDs.
Utility: Enables real-time monitoring, trend analysis, and data-driven capacity planning.
 
Policy Configuration Snippet:
 
<StatisticsCollector async='false' continueOnError='false' enabled='true'>
	<Statistics>
		<Statistic name='custom_idcorrelation' ref='idcorrelation' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idflow' ref='idiflow' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idpackage' ref='idpackage' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idsapprocess' ref='idsapprocess' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idsender' ref='idsender' type='string'>0</Statistic>
	</Statistics>
</StatisticsCollector>

Conclusion: Discipline Over Chaos

The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern provides the governance layerSAP API Management needs but doesn't offer out-of-the-box.

Benefits Recap:

Infrastructure:
- 96% reduction in proxies (20 → 4)
- Deployment: 15 min → 30 seconds - KVM
- Template-driven: 4× faster at scale

Governance:
- Centralized security policies
- Unified analytics per domain
- End-to-end traceability (SAP ALM ready)

Business Impact:
- Faster time-to-market for new integrations
- Reduced operational overhead
- Future-proof: Consumer contracts unchanged during backend migrations

 

Figure 30 - Implementing DCRP transforms your gateway into ascalable, semantic, governed ecosystem

The Roadmap Ahead

This is only the first step in mastering enterprise-scale integration:

  • Part I: DCRP– Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (Gateway Layer)
    👉YOU ARE HERE
  • Part II: PDCP – Package-Domain-Centric Pattern (Orchestration Layer): Solving CPI Package and User Sprawl ⏱️ COMING SOON
  • Part III: Testing & Monitoring** – DCRP + PDCP Validation with SAP ALM⏱️ COMING SOON

Before You Scale: Pre-Flight Checklist

✅Team Alignment: Ensure developers understand 1:1 → domain-centric shift
✅Stakeholder Education: Business sponsors grasp consolidation benefits
✅Establish Governance: Define KVM update approval workflows
✅Enforce Standards: Naming convention 
✅Sandbox PoC: Validate with 2-3 iFlows before production rollout

"In architecture, as in the field, discipline is not about restriction; it is about creating the boundaries that allow your forces to scale without chaos."— The Commander, Ricardo Viana

If this article helped you, please like, share, and reach out if you're implementing DCRP in your landscape!

Kind Regards,

Viana | SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert

entry.title: SAP Community Hobbies Series: Two Hearts, Four Paws: Growing Together Through Dog Agility
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

my name is Joker and I’m a mixed-breed dog, 4 years old… woof woof… ok, I’m almost 5. I’m here to tell you about one of my favourite hobbies, besides reading books.

 Hmm… woof woof…..ok, wrong photo.

 

Much better!
This hobby is called Dog Agility Sport, and I practice it together with my best friend @patty_1982 , whom you probably already know because I believe she’s quite well known in this community… woof woof… wooof…

 

............…sorry about that, my mischievous dog just stole my keyboard 😁😂

Here I am, I’ll take over from here.

My name is Patrizia Rossi, I’m a SAP Solution Architect, I have strong expertise in SAP Business Technology Platform, and I’m currently part of the IT team at the European Space Agency (don’t worry, I’m not the one going to the moon 👩‍🚀).
As you may have guessed, I’ve had a dog for almost five years now, and he has truly changed my life. Thanks to him, I discovered that I could do so many other things, not just the usual walks in the park. Four years ago, we started this journey to learn a wonderful sport: Dog Agility Sport.
Like all sports, it requires discipline and, above all, control of your dog. This control is essential because the dog must not get distracted by the surrounding environment, such as the presence of an audience watching, noises around the field, or even other dogs nearby. Throughout the entire run, the dog needs to stay focused on the handler, maintaining attention, trust, and connection from the first obstacle to the last. In this sport, Joker and I are what is called a team (or handler–dog pair). I have to say we were lucky, because there was already an incredible mental connection between us, and that really helped us grow together in this sport.

As I mentioned before, a strong connection is essential.
As a handler, you must guide your dog through the obstacle course, and the dog must understand what to do through your voice and your gestures. The field covers a large area and is made up of obstacles such as jumps, tunnels, slalom poles, the dog walk, and many others. The number of obstacles can go up to 22, although I can assure you that during training, we easily reach 28 😅. What you need, in addition to physical training, is a very good memory… and a solid dose of problem-solving skills 😎

Considering that in every training session or competition the course is always different, the first thing the handler must do is walk the course… without the four-legged friend. 🐕
You need to walk the field to understand the route the dog will have to follow and memorize it. During competitions, you don’t have many minutes to do this.

There are numbers that indicate the sequence of the obstacles, but they don’t explain how the dog should approach them: whether to turn around, enter from the front, from the back, and so on. That’s something you have to figure out yourself, by literally putting yourself in your dog’s paws. 😮 Yes, exactly: the point of view is the dog’s, not yours. Based on that, you must decide which voice commands or gestures to use.

Our training day is Friday evening: a wonderful way to end the working week and completely switch off. Nothing stops us neither rain, nor winter cold, nor summer heat.
This sport makes both of us feel good, because our main goal is simply to have fun. How do you teach a dog this sport? You need a lot of patience and some tasty snacks or appealing rewards. With dogs like Joker, training is done through shaping.

A bit like machine learning: step by step, the dog learns the mechanisms, and over time it takes very little for him to understand what to do. Very often, he even anticipates me! We are also very aware of our limits: Joker is a mixed-breed dog and will never be as lightning-fast as a Border Collie. But he’s truly great and learns very quickly. For example, it took some time for him to get used to the seesaw, because at the beginning he was scared by the noise the plank makes when it hits the ground during the descent.

That said, training is not only about teaching the dog, I also need constant learning and guidance. That’s why we work with a trainer, Elisa 😊, who helps us improve as a team. One of the most important aspects for the handler is understanding the right moment to indicate an obstacle to the dog, as well as maintaining correct body posture throughout the run.

Yes, that’s right: our body speaks to the dog far more than we often realize. If an obstacle is straight ahead but our feet are pointing in a different direction, the dog will take the wrong line. Or, if we open our arm to indicate a jump but fail to properly open our chest, the dog may interpret the signal differently and run behind the obstacle instead of jumping it. In agility, timing, posture, and body language are just as important as speed.
And yes, friends 😅 now imagine having to do all of this while running at full speed and, at the same time, trying to remember every single step of the course.

Our “flaw”, if you can call it that, is that we are so closely bonded that we can’t stay far apart. If I must send him into a tunnel from a distance, I can’t stay too far away, because he always wants me nearby 😉
This means I have to run more on the field, to stay as close as possible to the obstacles with him.
What I can tell you, though, is that all of this is rewarded in an invaluable way: when your dog looks straight into your eyes and shows you complete trust in being guided through this wonderful adventure.

 

 

 

Every now and then we also take part in competitions, but as I mentioned at the beginning, our goal has never been about trophies or podiums. For us, competitions are part of the sport: a way to test our training, to read a new course, to manage emotions, and to grow as a handler–dog team under pressure.
Our real success lies in the run itself: in every clear obstacle, in every decision made in a split second, in the trust that allows my dog to follow my lead without hesitation. It’s about stepping off the field tired but fulfilled, knowing we gave our best, learned something new, and strengthened our connection once again. And when, at the end of the day, we are already looking forward to training again the following Friday, that’s when we truly know we’re winning.

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: certified academy for SAP BTP Administrator
entry.content:
Let’s be honest, certifications can feel overwhelming.  You know the platform.  You’ve touched the systems.  Still, the exam looms, and the question pops up: Am I ready?

The Partner Learning Accelerator: Certified Academy for SAP BTP Administrator was built for that exact moment.

In this 6-hour live session, we slow things down just enough to make everything click.  From SAP BTP environment administration to security, identity services, and cloud operations, we connect real-world partner experience with what the certification actually expects.

The thing is this isn’t about cramming.  It’s about understanding.  About walking away thinking, “Okay—now this makes sense”.

If you’ve already worked with SAP BTP and completed the recommended learning journey, this academy can be the bridge between hands-on work and exam success.

Join us, sharpen your skills, and take that next step with confidence.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/nov/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00011998.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartnerCommunity #CertificationJourney #SAPEnablement

entry.title: Leveraging SAP Architecture Center for SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Good architecture doesn’t slow you down.  It removes friction.

That’s the idea behind Leveraging SAP Architecture Center for SAP Business Data Cloud—a sharp, partner-focused webinar kicking off our new data architecture miniseries.

SAP Architecture Center has just launched, with SAP Business Data Cloud front and center.  Inside, you’ll find solution reference architectures, diagrams, scenarios, service lists, and real examples—built to support how partners actually work.

In one session, we’ll:

  • Introducing the data architecture miniseries
  • Demo the SAP Architecture Center
  • Break down top SAP BDC use cases (data & analytics transformation, BW modernization, Databricks, AI)
  • Show how to reuse architectures in tools like draw.io
  • Explain how partners can contribute and shape what comes next

No fluff.  Just clear guidance to help you design better solutions, faster—and with confidence.

If you work with SAP BDC, this is worth your time.  Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010833.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPArchitecture #SAPBusinessDataCloud #SAPPartners #DataDriven

entry.title: From XSUAA to AMS: The Practical Path to Stronger Authorization
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as APJ Principal Cybersecurity Advisor and Compliance and has been recognized as a Fellow of Information Privacy (FIP) by the International Association of Privacy Professionals (IAPP). As part of his responsibilities, Jana helps with strategic customer engagements related to topics such as cybersecurity, data privacy, secure data flow, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit, and compliance.)

Introduction

SAP BTP security architecture is often explained in fragments: authentication here, authorization there, principal propagation somewhere else. But in real enterprise landscapes, these are tightly connected flows—and the exact sequencing matters. A small misunderstanding in where tokens are issued, how identities are propagated, or where decisions are enforced can quickly turn into inconsistent access, brittle integrations, and gaps in traceability, especially in hybrid (cloud + on-premises) environments.

In this blog, I’ll provide a comprehensive technical walkthrough of two complementary authorization models on SAP BTP, using numbered, end-to-end flows directly mapped to architecture diagrams:

  1. XSUAA-Based Flow — authentication, token routing, and principal propagation to on-premises systems
  2. AMS-Based Flow — centralized, policy-based authorization with CAP CDS enforcement

From there, we’ll zoom out to the “why”: where XSUAA fits best, why XSUAA alone can become limiting at enterprise scale, and what Authorization Management Service (AMS) adds—especially when you need consistent policy enforcement across applications, clearer separation of duties, and stronger auditability.

For the sake of clarity, this post intentionally stays at a high architectural level: I focus on concepts, sequencing, and decision points, and do not go into step-by-step configuration. The goal is to build a clear mental model you can reuse—before diving into implementation details in your own landscape.

XSUAA-Based Flow: End-to-End Identity and Principal Propagation to SAP S/4HANA (On-Prem)

This architectural pattern shows how a real end-user identity securely accesses an SAP BTP application and is then propagated to SAP S/4HANA on-premises—without falling back to shared technical users. That end-to-end identity continuity is a key foundation for zero-trust design principles and for auditable user accountability, especially in hybrid (cloud + on-prem) landscapes.

The goal is simple: the user who clicks in the browser is the same user recorded in SAP S/4HANA logs and authorization checks.

Note for clarity: This section focuses on the architecture and sequencing at a high level. Detailed configuration steps are intentionally out of scope to keep the mental model clear and reusable.

Systems involved

This XSUAA-based flow spans multiple components across cloud and on-premise boundaries:

  • Corporate Identity Provider (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, Okta) – enterprise authentication source (password, MFA, conditional access)
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) – federation proxy between corporate IdP and SAP BTP trust
  • XSUAA (Authorization and Trust Management) – OAuth2/OIDC token service for SAP BTP apps (JWT issuance, scopes, role collections)
  • AppRouter + Application Runtime – application entry point, session handling, and token forwarding
  • Destination & Connectivity – secure routing and token exchange for outbound calls
  • SAP Cloud Connector – controlled, outbound tunnel into the on-premise network
  • SAP S/4HANA (ABAP) – backend authorization enforcement using ABAP roles and authorization objects

Figure 1: Authentication, token routing, and principal propagation via XSUAA

 

Architecture diagram: XSUAA flow

 Authentication steps (blue phase)

Step 1 — User access

The user opens the SAP BTP application in a browser. AppRouter acts as the entry point and checks whether a valid session already exists.

Step 2 — AppRouter triggers authentication via XSUAA

If no session is present, AppRouter redirects the user to XSUAA (/oauth/authorize). XSUAA then redirects to IAS as the trusted identity provider—following the OAuth2 authorization code flow:

  • AppRouter → XSUAA (/oauth/authorize)
  • XSUAA → IAS (trusted IdP)

Step 3 — IAS federates to the corporate IdP

IAS forwards authentication to the corporate IdP. This ensures:

  • password/MFA and corporate policies stay with the enterprise IdP
  • no user credentials enter SAP BTP

Step 4 — Authentication assertion returned

After validation, the corporate IdP returns a signed assertion/token to IAS containing the authenticated identity and relevant claims.

Step 5 — IAS issues an SAP-trusted identity token

IAS validates the corporate IdP response and returns a SAML assertion (or OIDC response, depending on setup) that XSUAA trusts for user authentication. XSUAA then issues the JWT access token.

Step 6 — Authorization code exchange and JWT issuance

XSUAA exchanges the authorization code for a JWT access token:

  • includes user identity, scopes, and role collections
  • is forwarded to AppRouter and the application runtime
    At this point, authentication is complete and authorization context is represented in the token.

Token and routing steps (green phase)

Step 7 — Destination selected

When the app needs to call SAP S/4HANA, it resolves the target destination (host, authentication type, proxy settings) via the Destination service.

Step 8 — Destination token exchange for backend compatibility

Based on destination configuration, SAP BTP performs the required token exchange to produce a backend-compatible credential, such as:

  • OAuth2 SAML bearer / assertion-based flows
  • principal propagation material (cert-based)
  • short-lived, target-specific credentials (not reused across systems)
    Critically, the user context is preserved, enabling traceability end to end.

Step 9 — Connectivity routing boundary

The outbound request is routed through SAP Connectivity service, enforcing the key boundary:
on-premise systems are not opened for inbound access from the cloud.

Principal propagation steps (red phase)

Step 10 — Secure tunnel via SAP Cloud Connector

The request traverses into the on-premise network via SAP Cloud Connector, using mutually authenticated transport and explicitly configured trust. This ensures:

  • cloud identity is cryptographically verified
  • only permitted resources are reachable
  • connectivity remains controlled and outbound-initiated

Step 11 — Principal mapped in SAP S/4HANA

Inside SAP S/4HANA, the propagated identity is mapped to an ABAP user (for example via certificate mapping rules). The important outcome is:

  • no shared technical user is required
  • the user identity remains intact across the boundary

Step 12 — Authorization enforced in ABAP

SAP S/4HANA performs authorization using native ABAP mechanisms:

  • ABAP roles/profiles
  • authorization objects (e.g., S_TCODE, business objects, org-level checks)
  • application-level authorization checks

Outcome: SAP S/4HANA logs and authorization traces reflect the real end user, not a generic system account—supporting strong accountability and audit readiness.

Key security benefits

  • True SSO experience across cloud and on-premise
  • End-to-end audit trail mapped to real users
  • No shared credentials with broad access
  • Authorization enforced where data lives (ABAP stays authoritative for ABAP)
  • Clear trust boundary with controlled, outbound-only on-prem connectivity

Part 2: AMS-Based Authentication and Centralized Authorization

In Part 1, we walked through an end-to-end XSUAA-based pattern where a user signs in through the enterprise identity provider and the application receives a trusted access token for calling services and backends. Part 2 keeps the end-user SSO experience (Corporate IdP → IAS) consistent, while shifting authorization to centrally managed policies. Authorization decisions are driven by centrally managed policies, not scattered across application-specific logic.

With Authorization Management Service (AMS), policy definition and evaluation are externalized into a central service. Your CAP application then enforces those decisions consistently through CDS-based authorization (for example, using CAP annotations such as @requires and @restrict). This approach helps teams implement fine-grained and consistent authorization across services while improving governance and auditability because policies can be reviewed and managed in one place.

Note for clarity: This section stays intentionally high-level and architecture-focused. Detailed configuration steps are not included, so the flow is easier to understand and apply across different landscapes.

Systems involved

  1. This AMS-based flow spans the following components:
  2. Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Enterprise authentication source (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, Okta)
  3. SAP Cloud Identity Services
  4. Identity Authentication (IAS) – federation and login orchestration
  5. Identity Directory – user/group/attribute context used for policy evaluation (often synchronized from upstream identity sources)
  6. Authorization Management Service (AMS) Central policy store and policy evaluation engine
  7. CAP Application (with AMS integration) Application runtime that enforces policy outcomes via CAP CDS-based authorization (e.g., entities/actions annotated with @requires / @restrict)
  8. Destination Service Target-specific token exchange and connectivity preparation for backend calls (when required)

Architecture diagram: AMS flow

Figure 2: AMS authentication and centralized policy-based authorization flow

Authentication steps (blue phase)

The authentication phase remains the same as the XSUAA-based pattern—preserving the user experience and existing corporate IdP integrations.

Step 1 — End-user access The user opens the SAP BTP application. The application entry point (AppRouter + CAP runtime) checks for an active session.

Step 2 — AppRouter redirects to IAS If no session is present, AppRouter initiates sign-in using OIDC and redirects the user to Identity Authentication (IAS).

Step 3 — IAS federates to the corporate IdP IAS forwards authentication to the corporate identity provider using SAML or OIDC federation. Enterprise authentication controls (such as MFA and conditional access policies) are enforced at the corporate IdP.

Step 4 — User authenticates The user successfully authenticates with enterprise credentials. The corporate IdP validates the authentication request.

Step 5 — Assertion/response returned to IAS The corporate IdP returns a signed SAML assertion or OIDC response to IAS confirming the authenticated identity.

Step 6 — The application receives a trusted access token After successful sign-in, the application receives a trusted access token (commonly a JWT) representing the user identity and session context. The token is forwarded to the CAP application runtime.

Centralized authorization using AMS (green phase)

This is where AMS differs from a “token-only” approach. Tokens establish who the user is; AMS policies determine what the user is allowed to do and see. Instead of embedding authorization checks throughout each application, CAP can delegate policy evaluation to AMS and enforce the outcome consistently at runtime.

Step 7 —The CAP AMS integration (plugin) resolves the user’s assigned AMS policies and injects roles/filters for the request; CAP then enforces authorization via CDS rules.

Step 8 — Identity context and attributes resolved AMS builds the authorization context needed for evaluation, typically based on:

  • user attributes (e.g., department, region, cost center—depending on what your landscape provides)
  • group memberships (from Identity Directory and/or upstream identity sources)
  • policy assignments (which policies apply to the user and the requested resource)

Step 9 — Policies evaluated AMS evaluates centrally managed policies and determines the authorization outcome, such as:

  • permit/deny decisions for actions and services
  • fine-grained constraints and conditions (for example, patterns that can support entity/field/instance-level controls where applicable)

Step 10 — Decision returned and enforced in CAP The authorization outcome is returned to the CAP runtime and enforced consistently by the application:

  • decisions may be cached where appropriate for performance
  • only authorized requests proceed to data access and downstream calls This preserves a clean separation: policy is centrally managed and evaluated; enforcement happens reliably at runtime.

Backend access (purple phase)

Authorization is decided and enforced at the application/API layer first. If the authorized request requires backend access, the application then obtains target-specific credentials.

Step 11 — Target-specific token exchange (Destination Service) If the CAP application must call a backend (e.g., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Business Data Cloud, SuccessFactors, or internal BTP services), Destination Service performs the appropriate, target-specific token exchange based on the configured trust relationship—issuing short-lived, target-compatible credentials.

Backend systems continue to enforce their own native authorization controls. AMS primarily standardizes and centralizes authorization at the application/API layer to reduce drift across applications and improve governance.

Trust relationships in the AMS architecture

A typical trust chain includes:

  1. BTP subaccount with IAS tenant (trust configuration for authentication)
  2. IAS with  corporate IdP (federation via SAML/OIDC)
  3. Destination Service with target system (target-specific trust and token exchange)
  4. AMS with Identity Directory (groups/attributes and policy assignments used for evaluation)

Why AMS matters at enterprise scale

As landscapes grow, authorization logic often becomes inconsistent across apps: duplicated rules, different interpretations of “who can do what,” and uneven auditability. AMS addresses this by enabling:

  • Centralized policy management – less drift across multiple services and teams
  • Consistent enforcement – CAP applies policy outcomes uniformly via CDS-based authorization
  • Fine-grained control – more expressive policies beyond coarse-grained token scopes
  • Stronger governance and auditability – policies can be reviewed, versioned, and managed centrally

Part 3: When AMS Beats XSUAA-Only Authorization at Enterprise Scale

Choosing between “XSUAA-only” authorization and an AMS-driven approach is an important architectural decision in enterprise SAP BTP landscapes. The key is understanding what each service is optimized for:

  1. XSUAA primarily provides identity and token-based authorization context (roles/scopes represented in the token).
  2. AMS primarily provides policy-based authorization decisions: what a user is allowed to do, under which conditions, and why.

In practice, XSUAA is excellent for authentication and issuing trusted access tokens, while AMS is designed to scale authorization governance through centralized policy definition and evaluation—especially when requirements become dynamic, conditional, and organization-wide.

 

Note on technical accuracy: In CAP, you’ll still use authorization constructs such as @requires and @restrict. The difference is that CAP remains the enforcement point, while AMS centralizes the policy decision logic and governance, reducing duplication and drift across applications.

Problems AMS solves

1) Role explosion

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

XSUAA authorization is commonly modeled with role collections and scopes. At enterprise scale, access needs quickly diversify by region, business unit, data domain, sensitivity level, and job function. If you try to represent all combinations as static roles (for example, Sales_Manager_EMEA_Read vs. Sales_Manager_APAC_Write), the number of roles can grow rapidly—making access management harder to maintain, review, and keep consistent.

How AMS helps:

AMS supports policy-based authorization where access rules can be expressed in terms of attributes, groups, and conditions. Instead of multiplying static roles, you can capture intent once, for example:

“Sales managers can access sales data for their assigned region.” Access is then evaluated dynamically based on user attributes/group assignments and policy conditions—reducing role sprawl and improving governance.

2) Hard-coded or application-specific authorization

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

Even when CAP annotations are used, real-world authorization often becomes application-specific: additional checks in handlers, custom logic per service, and authorization rules scattered across multiple codebases. When access requirements change, updates frequently require code changes, retesting, and redeployment, which slows governance and increases the risk of inconsistent implementations.

How AMS helps:

AMS enables teams to externalize authorization decision logic into centrally managed policies. CAP can enforce those outcomes consistently, while policy changes can be introduced centrally—reducing the need for every application team to re-implement or duplicate the same authorization logic. This reduces coupling between policy evolution and application release cycles.

3) Inconsistent enforcement across services and channels

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

When each application implements authorization slightly differently, the same user can receive different access outcomes depending on which application or API is used. Over time, this creates gaps, exceptions, and “shadow rules” that are difficult to detect—and even harder to justify.

How AMS helps:

With AMS, authorization logic becomes centrally defined and can be applied consistently across CAP services and APIs that enforce the same centrally governed policy model. This improves uniformity of access decisions and reduces the risk of policy drift between applications.

4) Audit opacity: “Why does this user have access?”

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

When authorization is split across role assignments, token scopes, and application-specific checks, it becomes difficult to answer audit questions such as:

“Why does this user have access to this data?” The answer often requires correlating role collections, reviewing code paths, and interpreting differences across multiple services.

How AMS helps:

AMS makes authorization more reviewable and explainable because policies are expressed as centrally managed declarations. This improves audit readiness: reviewers can assess policy intent directly, track changes over time, and relate authorization outcomes back to policy logic—without needing to reverse-engineer authorization behavior from multiple application deployments.

Aspect

XSUAA

AMS

Primary purpose

Identity and trusted token issuance; token-based authorization context (roles/scopes)

Central policy-based authorization decisions and governance

Authorization model

Role collections / scopes (token-centric)

Policy-based (commonly enforced through CAP CDS authorization patterns)

Where rules live

Often split between role assignments + application-specific checks

Centralized policies, enforced consistently at runtime

Granularity

Typically coarse-grained (role/scope level)

More fine-grained and conditional access patterns (including instance/attribute-driven controls where applicable)

Change management

Changes often require adjusting roles and/or application logic

Many access changes (policy assignment/composition) can be handled centrally; changes to the underlying CDS authorization model still require normal application change control.

Consistency across apps

Can drift if each app implements checks differently

Reduces drift through centralized policy intent and evaluation

Audit readiness

Harder to explain “why” across roles + code

Easier to review policy intent and changes centrally

Part 4: How AMS Meets Compliance Requirements

SAP Authorization Management Service (AMS) is built for enterprise-scale authorization governance: central policy definition, consistent enforcement, and reviewable access intent. That combination maps well to common assurance and internal control expectations—especially where organizations must demonstrate who can access what, under which conditions, and how those rules are managed over time.

Separation of Duties (SoD)

Assurance context: In many organizations, SOC 1 and SOC 2 expectations (and internal control requirements) include demonstrating separation between individuals who can initiate, approve, and execute sensitive business transactions—particularly when those processes impact financial reporting or operational assurance.

How AMS helps: With centralized, policy-based authorization, SoD constraints can be expressed as explicit, reviewable rules (for example, “an approver must not approve their own request”) and enforced consistently at runtime—without relying on ad-hoc checks scattered across application code.

Principle of Least Privilege

Regulatory context: Privacy and security frameworks commonly require access to be limited to the minimum necessary. For example, GDPR Article 25 (“data protection by design and by default”) emphasizes default protections and limiting unnecessary accessibility.

How AMS helps: AMS supports least-privilege patterns through policy conditions such as:

  • Conditional access — allow only when business conditions are met (e.g., status, ownership, org scope)
  • Context-aware decisions — incorporate user attributes (e.g., department, region, cost center) to avoid broad entitlements
  • Time-bound access — model temporary access by policy condition (e.g., time windows) to reduce standing privilege

Result: access can be scoped precisely to job function and context, rather than granted broadly via static roles.

Auditability and transparency

Assurance context: In assurance engagements such as SOC 1 / SOC 2, organizations typically need controls to be documented, reviewable, and operationally verifiable—including the ability to justify why particular access is granted.

How AMS helps: Centralized policy-based authorization improves audit readiness because:

  • Policies are centralized — authorization intent is easier to review than logic distributed across multiple codebases
  • Policy lifecycle is governable — change management practices (review/approval, versioning, traceability) can be applied to policy updates
  • Reduced dependency on deployments — policy review can be performed independently of application release cycles, minimizing the need to “read code” to understand authorization intent

In short: it becomes much easier to answer, “Why does this user have access?” by pointing to centrally governed policy intent rather than piecing together multiple implementations.

Zero Trust alignment

Framework context: Zero Trust shifts away from implicit trust and toward continuous verification and explicit authorization decisions for each access request.

How AMS helps: AMS aligns well with core Zero Trust principles:

  • Authenticated — identity is established through corporate IdP federation (commonly via IAS in SAP BTP architectures)
  • Explicitly authorized — access is evaluated against centrally governed policy intent (no “implicit trust”)
  • Evaluated at runtime — authorization is applied at the time of access, not only at session start

This supports a practical Zero Trust posture where every request is evaluated against policy and context.

 

Framework / standard

Typical requirement

How AMS helps (capability)

SOC 1

Controls relevant to services affecting financial reporting (e.g., controlled access, segregation expectations)

Central policy intent, enforceable conditions, consistent authorization outcomes

SOC 2

Security and access control expectations (governed controls, reviewability)

Centralized policy governance and consistent enforcement

GDPR

Data protection by design/default; minimize unnecessary access

Least-privilege policies and contextual access scoping

NIS2

Risk management measures and access control expectations

Central governance of access rules and consistent enforcement

DORA

ICT risk management expectations; protection and prevention controls

Governable policy lifecycle and reviewable authorization intent

ISO/IEC 27001

Access control expectations; least privilege; reviewability

Attribute-/condition-based authorization patterns and centralized policy management

Conclusion

XSUAA remains a strong foundation on SAP BTP for establishing application trust and issuing tokens that carry identity and role/scope context in XSUAA-based architectures.

As landscapes grow—more apps, more teams, more data domains, and more audit scrutiny—authorization becomes the harder problem. Enterprise requirements increasingly demand consistent decisions, fine-grained controls, and a clear way to explain why access was granted.

This is where Authorization Management Service (AMS) strengthens the architecture by enabling:

  • Centralized, policy-based authorization that scales without role explosion
  • Improved governance support through consistent policy management and clearer traceability for audits (e.g., SOC/ISO) and regulatory expectations (GDPR, NIS2, DORA)
  • Clear separation of concerns: identity (who you are), authorization policy (what you can do under which conditions), and application enforcement
  • Consistent runtime enforcement in the application layer (e.g., CAP CDS @requires / @restrict) based on assigned policies and context

Future-proof security architecture for SAP BTP

  • Target state (new CAP apps): IAS for authentication + AMS for centralized authorization policy
  • Coexistence: XSUAA remains relevant for existing apps/scenarios; adoption can be incremental

In short: keep sign-in consistent, modernize authorization where it matters most, and scale governance without scaling complexity.

References:

SAP BTP Developer Guide: XSUAA to AMS migration

AMS vs XSUAA: The Future of Authentication and Authorization in SAP BTP

CAP Authorization Documentation

entry.title: Unpacking SAP Business Data Cloud for Solution Architects
entry.content:
Data everywhere.  AI expectations skyrocketing.  Architects caught in the middle.  Sound familiar?

In Unpacking SAP Business Data Cloud for solution architects, we cut through the noise and show how SAP BDC brings order to modern data chaos—fast.  One unified architecture.  Trusted data products.  Built-in governance.  And yes, AI and analytics that actually run on real business data.

In this session, you’ll get a sharp overview of SAP Business Data Cloud, why data products matter, and how capabilities like zero-copy sharing, object store persistence, and SAP Databricks integration fit together.  We’ll top it off with a live demo of an intelligent application and a walkthrough of the data product catalog, metadata, and lifecycle management.

Plus—and this is key—we’ll zoom in on partner best practices and where you can create differentiated value for customers.

If you design architectures, influence roadmaps, or sell data-driven solutions, don’t miss it.  Register now and save your seat.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010852.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPBusinessDataCloud #SAPPartners #DataAndAI #SolutionArchitecture
